meaning: A particle of emphasis or contrast 'but, however, on the other hand, as for'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. -s-, -si. Amh. -(ə)ssa, -(ə)s. Gaf. Arg. -s, Gur. -s; see Rundgren 1955: Index. The particle also occurs in Cushitic: Qua. -sõ, Kham. -s, Som. sa.

meaning: enclitic 'now!', '(I) beseech!'; e.g. 'adhana so 'save!, I beseech thee';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:116 interprets it as a demonstrative identifying it with s in 'asma, 'asku, soba. Rundgren 1955:44-8 analyzes soba as so-ba.
SEMITIC: perhaps SAr. s-t-b 'draw water' (W. Müller). cp. also Heb. ša'ab, Ug. š'b, Akk. šâbu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sa'aba 'follow'.
the root could be s'k, š'k, k'k.

meaning: 'ask, enquire, make a request, petition, invoke, beseech, intercede, pray, beg';
origin_explanation: mas'al 'petition, place of entreaty, place of prayer'; tasa'ali (Gr), part. of tasa'la; tasa'alo (t) 'question (n), dialogue'; SEMITIC: Ar. sa'ala 'ask', SAr. s'l, š'l, Heb. ša'al, Aram. š'el, Syr. š'e(l), L. Md. š'al, sa'alu. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)sä'ala 'ask', Har. (tä)sa'ala (tä)salä; in Amh. the root is preserved in (tä)salä 'make a vow', salät 'vow'.
from Syr. šiwul, Heb. šə'ol (Noeldeke 1910:34) in Tna.Amh. si'ol, Te. šə'ol, Gur. səwəl 'abyss, cliff'.

meaning: 'be unable, not find, be powerless, impotent, fail, (Lt) be in need of'; with indirect object suffixes: 'be impossible, not be possible';
origin_explanation: sa'un 'deprived of, impossible, indolent, impotent, powerless'; sa'nat 'inability, impotency, powerlessness, impossibility'; SEMITIC: the connection with Heb. šā'en in sa'anān 'at ease', suggested by Dillmann 377, is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Te. sa'ana 'not be able', Tna. sa'ana, Amh. (tä)sanä.
sa'ra mäy (Lt) 'skin bottle for water'; ETHIOPIC: Te. sa'ar 'skin bottle for milk or water', perhaps from Cushitic: Bil. si'ir, Qua.Af.Be. sär. Som. sa'ar.
probably misprint for k"a'asa.

meaning: Amh. garräta 'cut a vein in the forehead to bleed or cup a person, place a barrier across a road in order to impede passage'.

origin_explanation: see sa'at.

sa'al 'cough' (n); SEMITIC: Ar. sa'ala 'cough', SAr. s'l, Heb. š'l in hištä'el, Syr. š'al, Akk. sa'alu, salu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sä'ala 'cough', Te. sä'ala, Amh. salä, Gur. sal 'coughing'; passed into Cushitic: Kham. sal 'cough'.

'as'ama (Gr) 'allow to kiss'; tasa'ama, tasa'ama 'be kissed'; tasa'ama 'kiss one another'; 'astas'a'ama (DTW 1083), causative of tasa'a-ma; sa'ami (K) 'who kisses'; sa'mat 'kiss, kissing'; SEMITIC: Ar. (Yemen, region of Rayma) sa'am 'kiss' (W. Müller). ETHIOPIC: Tna. sä'ama 'kiss', Te. sä'ama, Gur. sa'a-mä, Amh.Gaf. samä; passed into Cushitic: Som. šum, Mo. šummi(ye).

meaning: 'remove with draw, cause to cease, undo, annul, destroy, cancel, rescind, abolish, dissolve, abrogate, dismiss, demote, discharge, depose, desecrate, violate, not observe (a holiday), break (an oath), frustrate, make null and void, bring to naught, cause to be of no effect, belie';
origin_explanation: sa'ara nafso 'he resigned himself'; 'as'ara, causative; also 'delete, destroy'; tasa'ara, passive; also 'resign onself, (Lt) desert'; sa'ari, act. part. of sa'ara; sa'ur, pass. part. of sa'ara; sa'ir 'abolition'; sa'rat 'dismissal, violation, abolition, abrogation, (Lt) removal, discharge, destruction'; sa'irot 'destitution'; mas'ar (Gr) 'abrogation'; tasa'rot (Lt) 'dismissal (from office), destitution'; SEMITIC: Ar. taġara 'break' (perhaps also Ar. šaġara 'deprive [of guardian or protector], expel, banish'), Soq. (y)ho'or (root š'r) 'split', Syr. t'ar 'break through' (for t'ar, Brockelmann 1908:276). Heb. šo'ar 'burst open'; von Soden 1118 compares Akk. ša'aru 'vanquish'. For 'gar' derived from this root see Koehler 1001, under ša'ar. ETHIOPIC: Te. sä'ara 'depose, abrogate (a law)', Tna. sä'ara, Amh.Gur. šarä 'dismiss, violate, de-pose', Har. šära; passed into Cushitic: Bil. sa'ar, Sa.Af. sa'ar, Qem. šaḥar.
from Gk. somareim, transcription of Heb. šo'arim 'vile (figs)'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (an)šäšä'a 'bubble'.

sa'atät 'canonical hours (in the night)'; sa'atät za-ma'alt 'canonical hours in day-time'; sa'atät za-lelit 'canonical hours in the night' (mashafa sa'atät 'Book of the Watch') (cp. also Chr.-Ar. kitabu-s-sa'at); for a bibliography, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:58. SEMITIC: Ar. sa'a(t) 'hour', Heb. ša'a, Aram. ša'a, Syr. ša'ta, Md. šita. According to Noeldeke 1910:44 Ar. sa'a(t) and G. sa'at are taken from Aram.-Syriac (so previously Delitzsch 41). ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. sa'at, Har. sa'a, Gur. sa'at, Amh. sat, sa'at. See also sa'a.
säb/bä 'green scum on stagnant water', perhaps from Cushitic: Bil. si'ir, Qua.Af.Be. sär. Som. sa'ar.
see also saḥbo.
perhaps from Ar. (Oman) sibā 'fortification' (W. Müller).

meaning: 'when, at that time, then'kama soba 'just as, when';'aska soba 'till then, until';soba + perfect, (occasionally) imperfect 'would that!';soba-hu introduces both real and unreal conditions; in an unreal condition the apodosis is introduced by 'am (see Dillmann 1907:551 ff)soba 'am + perfect 'even if';sobe (occasionally sabe) 'then, at that time, when';ya'ata sobe 'at that very time';sobe-ssa 'when, however';sobe-ha 'at that time, then, instantly, immediately, forthwith, thereupon, right away';soba-hi (Lt) 'as often as';sobe-hu 'when indeed';la-sobe-ha 'for the moment';'am-sobe-ha 'from that time forward, forthwith';'aska soba (Lt) 'even if'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sabu, sabu 'then' (Praetorius 1871: 44; Rundgren 1955:49ff); Dillmann 1907:405 analyzes soba as coming from so 'there' and ba 'in'; so also G. Bergsträsser, ZAW 29 (1909), 54 who derives so from a pronominal element ta. Rundgren 1955:44ff connects -be of sobe, with reservations, with the postposition -be of Harari. Hackenspill, ZA 11 (1896), 128 analyzes sobe as *soboy from *sobay and for the ending -ay he compares Ar. salā(y), nilā(y), Heb. 'ālē. Praetorius 1879:159, n.1 (followed by Brockelmann 1913:536) connects soba with Heb. šāb 'return'. Ar. tāba, G. soba meaning originally 'bei der Rückkehr, bei der Wendung zu einer Sache'. Reinisch 1875:107 (under hōb) identifies G. soba, sobe with Ar. sabba 'time' (so also Bittner, WZKM 23 [1909-10],408), soba in the accusative meaning 'at the time when, when'.

meaning: 'persons, men, people, mankind'; 'servants' (whether followed or not by bet 'house'); 'inhabitants' when followed by dagar 'city or country, state', or by the name of a city or of a country; followed by a noun it means 'those of..', as in sab'a 'ayn 'watchmen, spies', sab'a 'afrās 'horsemen, knights', sab'a masya'at 'tradesmen', sab'a sārāy 'sorcerers, magicians'sab'a (Y) 'become a man' (denominative);tasab'a 'become incarnated, become a man';sab'u 'who was made a man';subā'e (Gr.168) 'human nature, incarnation';sab'āwi 'human';sab'at 'relationship, affinity, humanity';sab'annā 'humanity, human nature, personal self';tasbā'e, tasbā'e 'human nature, incarnation';tasbā'ot 'assumption of human nature, manhood, incarnation of Christ celebrated the 29th of Maggābit.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Mh. habū 'people' (with h corresponding to s, š). Bittner 1909:20 expresses doubts concerning this comparison. ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna. sāb 'man', Tna. sābay, Te. sāba, Amh. sāb, sāw < sāb (for b > w, see Praetorius 1879:56; Cohen 1931:384ff), Gur. sāb, Gaf. sāwwa, Arg. su, Har. usu; probably passed into Cushitic. Som. sab 'lower class (of people)'.

meaning: 'make seven' (denominative)tasab'a, passive;sab'atu, sab'atu 'seven' (with a masc. noun);sab'u, sab'u 'seven' (with a fem. noun);sab'a 'a seven times';sābā'a (fem. sābā't) 'seventh';sābā'āwi, sābā'āy 'seventh';sab'u 'seven (days), the seventh day (of a week or of a month), week';sab'u (M) 'sevenfold';sab'a 'seventy';sab'a liqānāt 'Septuagint', lit. 'seventy learned men';subā'e 'week, seven-year cycle, seven days, prayer for a particular occurrence, retreat';subā'eyāwi 'pertaining to subā'e';masbā'it 'sevenfold, seven times';masbā'ita 'seven times, seven times as much, sevenfold';tasbā'at 'septuple';tasbā'a 'a seven times'.
origin_explanation: is connected with sb' I 'seven' reflecting the use of the number 'seven' in witchcraft formulas. For the connection between 'seven' and Heb. šb' (nif'al) 'swear', see Buhl 803. For a more detailed bibliography, see Landberg 1892:1892; cp. also Ar. (Syria) sab'a 'curse' (Landberg, ib.), Sh. esba' 'swear'.SEMITIC: Ar. sab' 'seven', SAR. s'b', Soq. hōbeh, Sh. šeb'et, Heb. šeba', Aram.-Syr. šob'a, Md. šaba, Ug. Ph. šb', Akk. sebe, šabe.ETHIOPIC: Te. sābu' 'seven', Tna. šob'atte, Amh. Gur. sābat, Gaf. sābattā, Arg. sa'int, Har. sātti. For 'seventy', cp. Te. Tna. sāb'a, Amh. sāba, Gur. sāba, Gaf. sāba. See also sb' II.

meaning: 'bewitch'sab'a (Lt) 'bewitch, make bewitch';sab', sab', sab'at (K*Lt), sab'at (T) 'bewitching, witchcraft, sorcery, spell, enchantment, incantation';masbā'i (K), masbā'i, pl. masbā'ayān (D) masbā'e (T) 'one who casts spells, wizard, magician, exorcist'.
origin_explanation: is connected with sb' I 'seven' reflecting the use of the number 'seven' in witchcraft formulas. For the connection between 'seven' and Heb. šb' (nif'al) 'swear', see Buhl 803. For a more detailed bibliography, see Landberg 1892:1892; cp. also Ar. (Syria) sab'a 'curse' (Landberg, ib.), Sh. esba' 'swear'.

meaning: 'be diffused, be spilled, be poured, spill (intr.), pour (intr.), be spread'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sābbābā 'pour, spill' (from Geez).

meaning: 'blow up'tasababa (T) 'be joyful';tasababa (MA) 'be completely inflated, be puffed up with pride';'asbāb (T) 'joyful, powerful'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. šubba 'be raised, be elevated'; cp. also Ar. šabba 'be lively, be sprightly, leap briskly'.
The meaning 'guard, sentinel' comes from the original meaning 'go around'.

meaning: 'turn, march around', Ug. sbb 'turn', Akk. šibbu 'girdle', Margoliouth, JRAS 1939, p. 59, derives Ar. sarāb of Surah 40:38 from G. sabaka and translates it 'guardhouses'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. sābaba 'plait', Tna. sābbābā 'bridle', sābab 'cord', Amh. šibbābā 'bridle', the original meaning being 'go around'. See also swb, soba.

meaning: 'look for a reason, look for a pretext' (denominative)sabab 'reason, cause, pretext'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. sabab 'cause, reason'; also Te.Amh. sābab.

meaning: 'viper, mythical beast with the body of a lion, tail of a snake, and the torso, arms and head of a man'
origin_explanation: from Ar. šibdī 'scorpion'; also in Amh. sābaddā'at (from Geez); see M. Cohen, BSL 31 (1931), 39-40. Strelcyn 1955:330. Noeldeke, WZKM 19 (1905), 404 thinks that Ar. šibdī is a Geez loanword.

meaning: 'praise, extol, laud, glorify, sing a hymn, magnify, honor'tasabbaḥa, passive;'asabbaḥa 'be made glorious';sabbaḥi, act. part. of sabbaḥa;sabbuḥ 'praised, glorified, celebrated, honored, glorious, full of glory, illustrious';sabbaḥo 'praise';sabbaḥe (pl. sabbaḥeyāt, sabbaḥāyāt) 'praise, hymn, lauds';sabḥat (Lt) sabḥet 'glory, glorification, praise, hymn, majesty, splendor';tasabbaḥi 'magnificence, majesty, splendor';tasabbaḥi 'praised';tasabbaḥot (Lt) 'glorification'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. šibbaḥ 'praise', Aram.-Syr. šabbaḥ. According to Noeldeke 1910:36, Ar. sabbaḥa and G. sabbaḥa come from Heb.-Aramaic. For Ar. sabbaḥa from Aram. šabbaḥ, see also A. Fischer 1953:52. ETHIOPIC: Te. sābaḥa 'praise' (v.), Tna. sabbaḥa 'praise' (n.); passed into Cushitic: Bil. sāb 'praise'.

meaning: 'smelt, melt down, cast molten images, make (an idol)'
preach, proclaim, announce, cry out, (Gr.168) teach
origin_explanation: tasabka, passive; sabaki (pl. sabakt, sabakayān) 'preacher'; sabak 'preaching, proclamation'; sabkat 'preaching, sermon, proclamation, promise'; (soma sabaka gennā 'Fast of Advent'; (ba'āla sabakat, or sabaka gennā 'preaching of Christ's birth, pre-Christmas feast'); masbāk, pl. masābkt (Lt) 'pulpit, (Gr.168, K*) verse of a Psalm sung before the reading of biblical passages appropriate to the holiday'; see also Velat 56; ETHIOPIC: Tna. sābkā 'preach, accuse'; Te. sābka 'go to a judge, accuse'; Amh.Gur. sābbākā 'preach', passed into Cushitic: Sa. sabak 'preach'.

transcription of Gk. sabakha, from Heb. sābakā. As for Semitic s-b-k 'interlace, interweave', cp. Ar. s-b-k- 'make like a lattice', Sb. esbak 'make a fence, make a net', Aram.-Syr. sābak 'interlace', Md. sbk, Akk. sabku. See also sābeq.

meaning: grow (ear of corn), become grain, ripen
origin_explanation: sabala, causative; sabul 'that has ears (corn), covered with ears of corn'; sabl 'ear of corn, grain that is still green but is ripe enough to eat'; sablat 'growing (ears of corn)'; sablannā 'formation of ears of corn'; masball 'growing (ears of corn), cornfield that produces ears of corn, (T.M) bunch, cluster'; SEMITIC: Ar. sunbula, sabal 'ear of corn', Sar. s.bl.t, Soq. seboleh, Heb. šibbōlet, Aram. šibbaltā, baltā, Md. sumbulta, Ug. šblt, Akk. šubultu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sābli 'ear of corn', Amh. sābl, sānbāl. See also sablaya, sanbal, sanbulā.

meaning: plant close together, weave together, connect, join
origin_explanation: perhaps reconstructed from sābeq (see below).
see also sanbilā.
the triradical sbl augmented by y; see sabla.
from Gk. sabanon 'linen cloth, towel'; also in Ar. subni, sabnītā, Syr. sabnītā, Ar. sabaniyya 'pièce de lin ou de coton, mouchoir de poche' (Dozy I, 630).

meaning: vine, called in Amh. aräg rēsa
origin_explanation: transcribes Gk. sabek, from Heb. sābak 'thicket'; also in Amh. adä sābeq, from Geez. See also sabaqa III, and sabake.
for a possible origin of the ending -qäkā it is worth mentioning that on the line below, T (p.158) has rägäkā with the same meaning as sobeqäkā.

sabbara 'break in pieces, shatter'; sabara, causative; also 'destroy, rout (an army)'; tasabra, passive of sabara; also 'flee (army)'; sabārt, act. part. of sabara; sabur 'broken, lame'; sabr 'fragment'; sabrat 'breaking (n), bruise, fracture, being broken'; sabbar 'piece, broken piece, fragment'; masbar, masbart, (Gr) masbar 'instrument for breaking, instrument of torture'; SEMITIC: Ar. tabara 'destroy, ruin, annihilate', Sar. tbr 'destroy, rupture', Soq. tiber 'be broken', Heb. šābar 'break', Aram.-Syr. tbar, Md. tbr, Ug. tbr, Akk. šeberu. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. sābärā, Te. sābra, Amh. sāb-bārā, Arg. sābbārā, Har. sābara. See also zabara I.
sabr (K*) 'patience'; from Ar. sabara 'bear patiently'; also in Te. sābr wādā 'be patient'.
transcription of Heb. šīwān.
misprint for silān; see salān.
sabāqi (K*), act. participle; transcription of Aram.-Syr. šəbaq 'abandon, leave'.

meaning: stir by rotating between the palms, (MA) strike
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sābäqā 'a 'mix, agitate', Amh. sāb-bāqā 'stir by rotating a beater between the palms'.

meaning: the koso tree from which anthelmintic medicine is made, tapeworm
origin_explanation: see also somarṭ, sorarṭ.

meaning: marry off (a son or a daughter in a religious ceremony), (MA) promulgate laws
origin_explanation: sabsāb 'marriage, matrimony'; (sabsāb 'married couple'); ETHIOPIC: Tna. sābsāb 'marriage', from G. (w)a-s-b-s-b 'marry' (see below), wsb becoming sb (with loss of w), reduplicated into sbsb. See also sabsaba II.
while there is some connection with sabsaba I, the relationship is difficult to establish.

probably denominative from *sb-t (šb-t), from sbb from which the reduplicative verb sābsāba 'intertwine' developed. Or would it be a denominative from Ar. sabat 'basket'? Note that Hava 305 considers Ar. sabat a Persian loanword.
from Heb. šəbāṭ.

SEMITIC: Sar. s.b.t 'beat', Mh. Hars. sebōṭ 'hit', Heb. šēbeṭ 'rod', Aram. šəbaṭ, Akk. šabbiṭu; perhaps also Ar. šabaṭa 'cause to cut up the skin' (note the irregular correspondence of the sibilants). ETHIOPIC: Tna. sābāṭ 'threshed grain', sābāṭṭ 'abbālā 'hit hard'; probably also Amh. šānābbaṭā 'chip, shave'.

meaning: banish, exile, drive forth, drive out, chase away, pursue, persecute, repel, cast out, cast forth, expel, exclude, reject, repudiate, excommunicate, depose
origin_explanation: tasaddada (K*), causative; tasadda, passive of sadada; sadādi, act. part. of sadada; sadūd, pass. part. of sadada; sadādī (Lt) 'persecution'; sadādāt 'exile, expulsion, persecution, oppression, distress, banishment, excommunication'; masdādī (instead of Dillmann 396 masadādī) 'one who drives away, one who repels'; SEMITIC: Ar. šādda 'persecute, treat severely, make an assault', but the phonetic correspondence with Heb. šādād 'deal violently', Akk. šadādu 'extract, remove' is not normal unless one compares Ar. šadda 'obstruct' > 'put in straitened circumstances,

meaning: 'pearl, mother-of-pearl'; from Ar. sadāf 'mother-of-pearl'; also Te.Tna.Amh. sadāf 'white shells which are worked into buttons, mother-of-pearl', Af. sadāf 'oysters'.
origin_explanation: from Ar. sadāf 'mother-of-pearl'; also Te.Tna.Amh. sadāf 'white shells which are worked into buttons, mother-of-pearl', Af. sadāf 'oysters'.
probably denominative from *sadāf 'butt of a rifle' that occurs in Te.Tna. Amharic.
occurs also as sadufawayān, name of God, for which see Strelcyn 1955:430.
in view of the form sadalawa it is tempting to connect the root with dalawa 'weigh' (see above), with the agglutination of the rare morpheme s occurring in the prefixed morpheme -as-.
SEMITIC: Ar. sadala 'rend a garment'.
perhaps a misprint for safādl (see below).
see sadalā 1.

meaning: (K) 'cereal spoiled by fog, kind of grass that grows in a field of wheat, (K) 'fields'.
origin_explanation: transcription of Heb. śǝdēmā, pl. śǝdēmōt 'special kind of tilled land, terrace' through Gk. sadmenōth.
SEMITIC: PBh. sǝdeq 'split' (n.), Aram.-Syr. sǝdaq 'split' (v.), Md. sdaq. ETHIOPIC: Gur. sǝdāqā 'split', Har. sǝdāqa to sataqa (see below).

meaning: 'make smooth, anoint, polish, smear, embellish, clothe, cover, spread'; sǝdāqā (K*), pass. participle;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sādāqā 'besmear, anoint copiously'.

meaning: 'compress, press together, smother, cram, accumulate, heap up'; tasadaqa, passive; sǝduq 'pressed together, compressed';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sānādāqā (augmented by n) 'pile, heap up (sheaves)'. Note, however, that in the quoted passage of Cant.2:5 the corresponding verb in the Hebrew text as well as in the Septuagint means 'refresh, comfort', and it could then be identified with sadaqa II.

probably from Amh. sādādārā 'put in order' or Gur. sādārā. In other Semitic languages: Heb. seder 'arrangement, order', Aram.-Syr. sǝdar 'arrange', Md. sdar, Akk. sadāru, sedēru. Barth 1893:56 compares Ar. sarada (with metathesis) 'put in regular order'.

meaning: one of the nails with which Christ was crucified;
origin_explanation: goes back to SATOR (through Coptic sador) used in the form of a cross (see Euringer, ZS 6[1928].304-5). See also alādor, arodā, dānād, rādās. For a bibliography dealing with the nails, see Euringer, ib., Strelcyn 1955:80, n.1, Dobberahn 135, 184, no.39.

meaning: 'make six' (denominative); tasaddasa (K*) 'become six'; sǝds 'six' (used with a feminine noun); sads-a 'six times'; sassu 'six'; sǝddastu 'six' (with a masculine noun); sads 'the sixth day (of the week or month, six days';

'sixfold'; saddāse (K*) 'making (n) six'; sǝddǝs (fem. sǝddǝst) 'sixth'; sǝddǝs (fem. sǝddǝsāwit), sǝddǝsāy (fem. sǝddǝsāyit) 'sixth'; sǝddǝsā 'sixty'; tasdast (K*) 'sixfold'; masdast (K*M) 'sixfold'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sitt 'six', sādīs 'sixth', SAr. s₂dt 'six', Sh. šitt, Heb. šeš, šēš, Aram. šet, Syr. šitā, vešitā, Md. šit. Ug. štt (?), Ph. šš, Akk. šeššet 'six', šedištu 'sixth'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. šǝddǝštā 'six', sǝdsa, sassa 'sixty', Te. sat 'six', Amh.Arg. sǝddǝst, Gaf. sǝddǝstā, Har. sǝddisti, Gur. sǝddǝst. For Tna. šǝdš (with š as against sds of the other languages), see N.V. Yushmanov, "A sibilant anomaly in Tigrinya numerals", in Africana 1 (1937).77-79.
see sadada.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. sāyyādā (with metathesis) 'be beautiful'. See also sadaya II.
SEMITIC: Ar. sdy, in saddādā, asdā 'confer benefits on someone' (Ambros); related to Akk. sēdu 'help'. ETHIOPIC: Te. sāda (sdw, sdy) 'help'. See also sadaya I.
transcription of Gk. saddai, from Heb. šadday, name of God.
see safaya.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sāfādal 'afterbirth'. See also safald, sǝdlw, asfādlawā.

SEMITIC: Ar. saffa 'skim over, fly close to the ground', Syr. šap 'crawl, walk softly', Akk. šapāpu 'move along' (?). ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāfāfā 'float', Amh. sāfāfā, tāsafāfā. For 'scum, froth', cp. Tna.Amh. sāfāf. The root passed into Cushitic: Kham. sif 'float'.
probably a denominative from safaf 'scum, froth', the verb having the meaning 'take off the scum, clear the scum > purify'.

meaning: 'stretch out (intr.), spread out, increase, be diffused, abound, be filled up, be crammed'; safuf 'expanded, extended, stretched out, wide open, diffused';
origin_explanation: perhaps related to sfh (see safha, below) > sf > sff.
from Gk. sphageion 'bowl for catching the blood of a victim in sacrifice'.

meaning: 'stretch out, extend, spread out, expand, lay open, make broad, enlarge, widen, (Y) be cured, (Lt) be successful'; asfǝha, causative; tasafha, passive, intransitive; also 'be covered over, (Lt) be successful'; sǝfuh 'wide, broad, outstretched, flat, large, spacious, spread out, extended, ample; (transferred meanings) open, sincere, guileless, generous, sound'; (sǝfuha 'generous'); (ba-sǝfuh 'generously'); sǝfh 'expansion, spaciousness, great extent, breadth, width, capacity, sincerity, simplicity; warp (of a loom)'; sǝfh (T.M) 'warp (of a loom)'; sǝfhe (T.M) 'plain'; sǝfhat 'spreading (n), expanse, extension, width, capacity, (Lt) infinity'; masfǝh 'outspread place, extension, place for expansion, (T) gathering place, public place'; masfǝh 'surface, measure; hammer, mallet, anvil'; masfǝh 'outspread place, extension, place for expansion, (T) gathering place, public place'; mastasfǝh 'extension, expansion';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. safaha 'pour' > 'libation'.

meaning: 'hit, hammer, work with a hammer, (H 217) insert a wedge'; safälyä, safälyä, see below; masäfäl, pl. (Lt) 'hammers'; see safala.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar.(Dat) säfila 'anvil', Yem. sufle, that is, 'that on which one hits with a hammer', even though the Geez noun refers to the hammer.

meaning: 'hammer, (T) instrument for breaking, wedge'; see safala.

'prevail, dominate, be master, rule, become powerful, exercise control, direct, (Y.K) be wide, be numerous, be full'; safana 'cause to be leader'; 'asfana, causative of safana; safäni, act. part. of safana; also 'ruler, governor, chief, controller'; safun 'powerful, prevailing, chief'; safn, see below; safnat 'principality, (K*) large number, fullness'; masfan (pl. masäfänt) 'ruler, governor, prince, chief, prefect, high official, judge, magistrate, captain'; (masfana 'awd 'judge of assembly'); (masfana mä'i 'centurion'); (vorit za-masfänät 'Book of Judges'); masfännä 'leadership, governing, ruling, princedom, lordship, office of master'; (hagära masfännä 'capital city');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. sapânu 'overpower' (so Barth, ZA 3 [1898], 60; also Rundgren 1955:26, n.2). The connection with Heb. säpan 'cover over' by Buhl 550 and Koehler 664 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Amh. säfänna 'dominate'. Praetorius 1879:81 compares sfn with šnf 'be victorious, vanquish': Tna. šännäfä, Amh. (a)šännäfä, fä. passed into Cushitic: Qua. safan 'be master'. See also msfn.

meaning: 'some, several, how many, how much?'; safna 'how often?, how many times?'; safnat (L) 'quantity';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1907:361 states that the original meaning 'prominence, size' (from safana, see above) came to be used as an interrogative in the sense of 'how much?, how great?'. The original form is 'asfantu from 'a-säfäntu, properly 'what is the it?, i.e. 'how much?'; then without the interrogative the form became säfna > safn. Praetorius 1890:371 likewise derives safn from 'asfäntu. The explanation of 'asfäntu is different from that of Dillmann (see 'asfäntu).
from Gk. spongos 'sponge' through Ar. sifung (si-fang) , safang (safang); also PbH. sapôg; in Ethiopic: Te.Tna.Amh. säfnäg.

meaning: 'Amh. yazzo käl 'a sour or bitter beverage, a resinous substance derived from wheat, grapes, or figs';
'grow thickly, grow densely'; a secondary form of safaqa (see below).

'measure, measure out, mete out'; 'asfara, causative; tasafara, passive of safara; tasafära 'take a share (in a thing), distribute, mete out, measure with others'; safäri, act. part. of safara; safr, pass. part. of safara; safr 'measure, size'; masfart 'measure (n), dimension'; masfar, masfart (pl. masfartät, masäfär, masfärt) 'measure, measurement, measuring line, (Lt) means of measuring (such as bowl, cup, basket), dimension'; masfart 'meter (in poetry)'; masfär 'measure, (K*) means of measuring';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. (Hadr) sufra 'board' (W. Müller, ZDMG 131[1981],402), Sär. sfr-t 'extent'. Heb. säpar 'count'. Ug. spr, Ph. m-spr 'number'; connected with the meaning 'recount' in Heb. sipper; see also Marrassigni 1971:71-2. Barth 1893:25 connects the root with Ar. sabara 'probe, measure'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Gur. säfärä 'measure'. Te. säfra, Amh. säffärä, Har. säfära; passed into Cushitic: Sa. safar 'measure'.

meaning: 'camp (v.)'; safar (K*) 'camp, encampment, section of town'; masfariyä (Lt) 'place of camping' (an Amharic form);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: connected with the root sfr 'travel': Ar. säfara 'travel', Soq. sfor. For its connection with Akk. supûru 'fold, cattle pen', see Marrassigni 70-2 who explains it as being 'un antico recinto o palizzzata di protezione di un accampamento'. The connection with safara I 'measure' suggested by Fisher 147 and Appleyard 55 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur.Har. säfär 'village, section of town'. Praetorius, ZDMG 64 (1910), 622 states that G. safar is an Amharic loanword. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa safar 'camp'.
from Gk. sphaira 'sphere'; also in Syr. säpirä 'ball, round', Aram. säperä, Md. spira.
from Ar. safrä 'gall, bile', lit. 'yellow'; also Amh. safra. See safr, and 'asfar, zäfrä.

meaning: 'stomach ache, colic'; perhaps to be identified with säfrä, above.

origin_explanation: from Gk. sappheiros 'sapphire', of Indian origin; also in Amh. sofor, Te. säfiros; in other Semitic languages: Ar. safir, Syr. sappirä, Aram. samfo-rinon, sanforinon, 'aspôr. For Heb. sappir, see Ellenbogen 125, Rabin 1968:434. See also sanper, below.
from Gk. sphairidia (nom. acc. pl. of sphairidion) 'little ball'.
from Ar. safargal (of Persian origin); also in Amh. säfärgal; in other Semitic languages: Syr. 'esparrgalä, Aram. 'ispargalin, Md. spargla. For the fruit, see Löw 144.

meaning: 'mislead, deceive, seduce, entice, persuade, beguile'; 'asfata, same; tasafata, passive; 'astasafata 'allow to be seduced'; safäti, act. part. of safata; also 'deceiver, seducer'; safut, pass. part. of safata; also 'wicked, deceitful'; safätit 'deceitfulness, deception, inducement'; saftat 'deceitfulness, seduction, enticement'; 'asfäti 'seducer, deceiver'; 'asfato(t) 'deceit'; masfät (K*) 'means of deceiving, seducing, enticing';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. säfatä, säfätä 'deny, cheat'; also säfäfä (with metathesis), perhaps Te. säfäfä 'a person that causes difficulties'. Amh. säffätä 'deny falsely'; also in Cushitic: Or. saffata, safata 'deny having received something'. Reinisch 1887:317, Wajnberg 1937:34, and Rundgren, OS 2 (1953),19-25 identify the Ethiopic root sft, šft 'deceive' with Heb. šapat 'judge' (v), Ug. tft.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. säfätä, säntät 'abälä 'touch lightly, skim the surface'. Amh. suffačč 'obsidian, splinter'.

meaning: 'hope, hope for, set hope (upon), have confidence, look for, look forward, await, wait, expect'; see tasaffawa.
bow down, prostrate oneself, pay homage, adore, worship by prostration
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. sǝgēd ‘prostrate oneself’, Heb. šāgād (Wagner 85), Ar. sagāda (G. sagada (Schwalley, ZDMG 52[1898].134, Noeldeke 1910:36). ETHIOPIC: Tna. sǝgādā ‘prostrate oneself’, Te. sǝgādā, Amh. sǝgādā, Har. sǝgādā, Gur. sǝgādā, passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. sagad. On G. masgid, see above.

SEMITIC: Heb. sǝgād, Aram. šigdā, Syr. šegdā Md. šigdā, Ph. šqd, Ug. šqd, Akk. šiqdu, šiqdu. For šqd, šqd, see Löw 374. The original root seems to be šqd, šqd (with q) becoming šgd by assimilation of qd > gd. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. sǝg(g)ād ‘kind of tree’. According to Noeldeke 1875:39, n. 3, G. sǝgād is an Aramaic loanword.

SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. sǝgīd ‘languid’ (Dillmann 399); however, sǝgīd in the meaning ‘languid’ seems to be used only in the expression ‘ayn sǝgīdā ‘languid or languishing eye’. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sǝgīd ‘d-am ‘dejected, dull, glum’.

phonetics: sogih, sogih ፳፯ (T.M), sogih ፳፯ (K), sogih ፳፯ (D 1402)
origin_explanation: transcribes Heb. šoge(h) ‘fool, who errs’.

meaning: 'give hope, cause to hope, promise, put trust'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Akk. suppu ‘implore, beg’; also SAR. s₂p₂t ‘promise’ (denominative from a noun *s₂f-t, from s₂fw). The comparison with Heb. šippā (spw) suggested by Dillmann 407 (so also Barth 1893:53) is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (a)sāfāwā ‘give hope’, Te.Tna. Amh. Arg. tāsfa ‘hope’ (n.); passed into Cushitic: Bil. Qem. tasfa, Had.Tem. tāssa.

SEMITIC: Ar. isfā (from sƒy) ‘awl’, Šh. sfe (sfw) ‘sew leather’, Akk. sāpū ‘bind’; related to Ar. saffa (sff) ‘weave leaves of a palm tree’. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāfāyā ‘sew’, Te.Har. sāfa, Amh. Arg. sāffa. Denominative verbs from *sfi: Te. sāfāta, Tna. sāfātā (Reinisch 1887:296). For ‘awl’, see Tna. māsfe, Te. māsfe ‘big needle’, Amh.Arg. wāsfe, Har. wāsfi, Gur. wāsfi. For the instrumental with w, see Cohen 1931:389-90. The root is common with Cushitic: Sa. safo ‘basket, wicker-work’, Bil. Qua. sabā, Qem. sāwā, while in languages, such as Mo. šippi-ye ‘sew’, Gim. sip, the root is taken from Amh. sāffa. If ‘basket’ is to be connected with the root ‘sew, weave’, cp. also Te.Tna. sāfī ‘basket’, soq. mǝsefi, Ar. (Oman) suff. See also masfet.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sagg`a ‘There is probably a connection with Tna. sagg`a ‘clan’; G. sagg`a would indicate someone who is known by the name of the clan.

phonetics: sag`a ፳፯ ፳፯. (T) sagu`a ፳፯ ፳፯
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāg`e, sāg`e ‘cut off, pierce’; related to sak`a ‘string, pierce’ (see below). The connection with Amh. sānāgga ‘castrate’ which Praetorius 1879:98 suggests, with reservations, is doubtful.
from Ar. sigillāt, of Latin (sigillatus) origin (Fraenkel 48).
from Ar. sigillāt, sagulāt ‘jasmine flower’, of Persian (sagulāt) origin.

SEMITIC: Soq. škimoh ‘grain of millet’. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. sagam; probably from Cushitic: Bil. sekmā ‘grain of barley’, Kham. sikemā, Aw. simeki. Praetorius 1879:95 derives it from gābs > *sāgm > sagam, sagam, but on p. 58 he derives gābs from sagam.
Dillmann 398 considers it a variant of sahm. K 849 identifies it with sagam ‘barley’, the arrow having a tip like the barley.

SEMITIC: Ar. asgam, razgām ‘camel that does not produce a sound’, Ar. (Yemen) sagğām ‘half close the eyes’ (Rossi 223), PBH. Aram. siggem ‘join, unite’. ETHIOPIC: Reinisch 1885:125 compares Qua. šigem ‘be silent, hold one’s tongue’.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sāggānā 'be handsome'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. sāgān 'ostrich', Tna. sāgān, sāgān, sāg'ān, Amh. sāg'ān; from Cushitic: Bil. sāgān, Tna. sāgānāt. Kham. sagūnā, Qua.Aw. sagānā (so also Brockelmann 1950:25).
probably from Amh. sāgännāt 'watchtower'; also in Tna. sāgännāt.
Dillmann 398 states that sag'ara is a variant of sag'arā. In fact the Asmara Bible has sag'arā (Ez. 23:25), for which see below.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. sag'ǝrti, šongurti 'onion', Te. sagurt, sagar 'leek', Amh.Arg. šankurt 'onion', Amh. also šong'art (d'Abbadie), Gaf. šankurt, Har. šankurta; also in Cushitic: Bil. šungurti, Dem. šingurči, Qab.Al. šunkurta. Dillmann 398 derives G. sag'ǝrd from Gk. skorodon 'garlic'. Brockelmann 1950:300 thinks that the Semitic-Ethiopic root is borrowed from Cushitic. Conti Rossini 1912:246 seems to be of the same opinion. See also Beguinot 521.
seems to be a misprint in comparison with sotāsas 'euphoria', the ጤጥ of which was misread for ግግ.
see also sabo.
see also sabo.
SEMITIC: perhaps related to šbb: Ar. šabba 'burn (fire)', Heb. šābīb 'spark', Aram. šābībā, Md. šambībā, Akk. šabābu, "etwa 'glühen'" (von Soden 1118).
fem. of *sahul, from Ar. sahil, sahl 'smooth, easy'.
from Ar. sahm 'arrow, share, lot'; also 'lot', Som.Af. sāmi. See also samhạm, sagam II.
perhaps connected with salapa I, sarapa by confusing the letters h, l, r.
is there any connection with sarapa I, below, by confusing the letters h and r?
Amh. zanb 'fly'.
from Amh. ši, šoh, of Cushitic origin.

meaning: 'draw, pull, drag along, lead, catch, attract, summon (demons), lure, protract, draw out';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sahaba 'drag, pull', Soq. shob, Heb. shāb, Md.Ph. shb. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāhabā 'drag, pull', Te. sāhaba, Arg. sāhaba, Amh.Gaf.Gur. sabā; probably also Har. sāhaba.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tā)sāhagā 'pant', Gur. (a)sagā 'pant, sob'. See also sahaga II.

meaning: 'be emaciated, be used up, be worn out, be coarse, become worse'.
origin_explanation: could possibly be combined with sahaga I, that is, 'be emaciated through sorrow', as in Amh. g'ādaggālā 'become thin because of difficulties or misfortune'. See also masag.

meaning: 'insert, stick in, enclose (livestock), tie onto (in making a roof, a mat)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. sāhag 'rope with which the tongue of a sacrificial animal is tied to the lower jaw', Amh. sagā 'insert', masag 'enclosure for animals', mashag (from Geez).

meaning: 'be coarse, be rough, be rugged, be scabrous, become hoarse, (T) be itchy, be peeled off here and there';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1908:273 compares it with Ar. hasaka 'rough thing, thistle' (with metathesis).

SEMITIC: Ar. sahala 'shave off, peel, scrape off', Sh. shal 'scratch, grind a knife' (probably from Arabic), Akk. šēlu 'sharpen'; to be connected with Heb. šelah (with metathesis) 'javelin', Ar. silāh 'weapon', Ug. šlḥ 'sword'. For the relation between 'be sharp' and 'javelin, spear, sword', cp. Te. bālāh 'spear' from blh 'be sharp'. For Indo-European, see Buck 1390. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāhalā 'sharpen' (also sāllaha 'be sharp'), Te. sāhala, Amh. salā, Gur. salā 'locally made razor'. A secondary form from *sahul 'sharp' > šul developed in Amh. šolā 'be sharp', Arg. šola, Gur. (Zway) šulu 'peg, sharp point'; passed into Cushitic: Be. sehal 'sharpen', Kham. sahel, sil.
variant of zuhal (see below).

meaning: 'be dry, be hard like a razor or iron';
'wear out by use, rub out by treading, become dried out, wither';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. sll in maslūl 'consumptive'; in this case G. shll would be augmented by h. ETHIOPIC: perhaps shll augmented by h, and cp. Amh. sāllālā 'be withered, paralyzed', Tna. sālālā, Te. sālala 'paralyzed'. For 'be dried out', see also sāhlala, above, and shlw.

meaning: 'go to and fro, move here and there, be incited, dance and sing, arrive suddenly'
origin_explanation: The basic meaning is that of movement; perhaps a root sll augmented by h; Amh. sulləl alä, täns'alälä 'run here and there', Tna. sälallä bälä 'dance'.
the form indicates 'something pertaining to sähəl'.

meaning: 'move backward and forward, agitate, rub, scrape, brush the teeth'; tasähəsäha, passive; 'ansähəsäha, ansahasaha 'move (tr.intr.), be in motion, move hither and thither, stir, arouse, (T) roam about, move backward, (K) provoke, instigate'; 'ansahasähə, act. participle; nasahsäh, (K) nasahsäh 'motion backward and forward, movement, agitation, stirring, com-motion'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sahsahə 'rub two things against each other'; also in Cushitic: Sa. säh 'agitate', Bil. sah 'y', Qua. sag. Conti 1980:89 compares it with the Semitic root with initial n: Akk. nasāhu 'tear out, uproot', Heb. nāsah 'tear away', Ar. nasaha 'delete'.

meaning: 'make a mistake, err, sin, do wrong, go astray, miss (a target, an aim), lose (the way), get lost, be misled, be seduced, be deceived'; 'ashata 'lead astray, lead into sin, induce into error, tempt to evil, corrupt, seduce, mislead, deceive, delude'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sahta, act. part. of sahta; sahūt 'who is in error, erring, misguided, induced into error'; sāhət 'error, sin'; saḥīt 'error, deceit'; sahtat 'error, transgression, going a take, seduction'; 'ashātī, mashatī, part. of 'ashata; 'deceiver, seducer'; mashət 'seducer, deceiver, erring, impostor'; mosāhət (KG) 'ruin, damage'. SEMITIC: SAr. sḥt 'defeat, throw into', Heb. sāhāt 'spoil, ruin', Aram. Šḥt 'be mutilated', Ph. šḥt 'ruin'; perhaps also 'extirpate, eradicate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. säḥata 'be mistaken, do wrong', Tna. säḥatä, Amh.Gur. satä, Arg. säḥata; pa Cushitic: Bil. sahat.
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. sayḥ 'striped garment', CUSHITIC: perhaps also Kham. si-t 'dress, dress', Qem. say, Aw. si, Bil. sa-s 'clothe someone from an ancient syh'.

meaning: 'wound, injure, molest, hurt, do harm, (a virgin), touch, pinch, scratch, prod, (up a quarrel), provoke ire'; tasahəta (K*), passive; sahātī, act. part. of sahata; sahūt, pass. part. of sahata; sahtat (K*), verbal noun of sahata;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. šaḥat 'oppression, corruption' JTS 34[1938].40), Syr. šaḥhet 'wound'. The con son with Akk. šēṭu 'disregard' suggested by von Soden 1221 is valid only if 'disregard' is considered a metaphoric usage of 'wound'. Driver, ib., also compares Akk. šāḥāṭu 'spring, mount (esp. sensu obsceno)'. The connection with Heb. šāḥaṭ 'slaughter', Ar. saḥata, Ug. šḥt, Akk. šaḥāṭu 'tear down, snatch away' should not be excluded. ETHIOPIC: Te. säḥata 'wound', Har. sēḥata 'insert ', Amh. satä, satä 'fit in, wedge', Amh. 'stab, insert', Gur. satä 'make penetrate (e.g. a comb into the hair)'. See also sahata II.
probably to be identified with sahata I, developed from the meaning 'incite a quarrel' > 'spread, separate, disseminate'.
for this tree, see Strelcyn 1973, no.68.

meaning: 'be extended, be expanded, be spread out, be dispersed, be scattered, (K*) run (liquid), (T) be dragged, drag along, crawl, (K*) languish, move slowly, drift (cloud, mist)'; note that T 160 makes a distinction between sahwa 'drag' and sahwa 'be dragged'; (sahwa labbu 'he lost courage'); 'ashawa 'extend, expand, spread out, (K*) pull, drag, (T.K) take someone take off his clothes'; sahāwī (K*), act. part. of sahwa; sahāw (K*) 'scattered, dispersed'; sāhw 'extension, dissolving, spreading out, expansion, (K*) drifting of mist, fine mist together with a cloud'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. šāha 'be open', šāha 'large, spacious'. ETHIOPIC: Te. säḥa (shw) 'be extended', Tna. säḥa-wä 'be scattered, be thinly sown'.
sahya (Y) 'disappear, be lost, be extinguished, (D 1395) separate'.
'asähaya 'dry' (tr.). also read sayyaha; see sayyaha II, below.

meaning: 'be wounded, be hurt, (Y.T) rub, scrub, massage'; sāhaya (T) 'incite to hate each other' (connected with 'wound, hurt'); Amh. sahäya (T.M) 'wound, hurt'
origin_explanation: there is a slight inconsistency in the Amharic translation of q''ässälä and g''ädda given by T for sahaya. Indeed, q''ässälä 'be wounded' is a verb of state, while g''ädda 'harm' is a transi-tive verb; SEMITIC: Ar. saḥā (shy) 'take off by scraping', Heb. šāḥa (nif'al) 'be scraped away'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. säha, säha 'blemish, imperfection, defect'.

meaning: 'warm oneself, become warm'; 'ashana (K*Gr) 'warm, heat, kindle a fire'; səhūn (K*) 'who warms himself'; səhīn 'incense, frankincense' (the incense pro-ducing a sweet odor when burnt); mashənať 'kitchen'; mashənať 'pot, (Lt) oven'; mashanīn (M.TS) 'incense' (seems to be a mis-take for səhīn);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. saḥuna 'become hot', Šh. šan 'warm oneself', Heb. šāḥīn 'inflamed spot, boil', P.Bh. šā-han 'become hot, grow warm', Aram-Syr. šaḥen, Md. šhn, Ug. šhn (Gordon 489, Caquot 347, n.1). Akk. saḥānu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säḥanä 'be hot', Te. säḥana; Amh. saḥīn 'incense' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Sa. saxna, saxnä 'scab'.

meaning: 'thread (a needle), string, arrange in a row, (Lt) pierce (that is, pass through an opening to thread), insert into a hole or crevice'; tasak'a, passive; also (Lt) 'be entangled'; sak'u 'threaded, interlaced, (Lt) fashioned (gold), processed for decoration';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) šakar 'thread, string'. ETHIOPIC: Te. säk'a 'thread', Tna. säk'e, säk'e, Amh. säkä, säk'a. The root is related to skk. Semitic: Heb šākak 'weave together', Syr. sakkek 'fix nails in', Akk. sikkatu (from skk) 'nail'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. säkākā 'thread through, drive through', Har. säkākā 'string, put in a row', Gur. säkākā 'drive a peg or a pointed object into the ground'. In this case sk' would have become reduced to sk and augmented by the last radical. The root is common with Cushit-ic: Qua. saq 'weave', Bil. saq, Kham. saq.
SEMITIC: Ar. sakka (skk) 'cut off (the ears)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säk"a "a 'crush'. See also säk"ayä, sas(s)äk, below.

meaning: 'defor-est, make a clearing'; with the enigmatic ending -läwa of which there are several examples in Tayyä.
origin_explanation: with the enigmatic ending -läwa of which there are several examples in Tayyä.
see sak"a-t.
see also sankärur.

meaning: 'kind of ant'; in Job 4:11, where the Hebrew text has layiš 'lion', and the Septuagint has 'ant-lion'; perhaps also sa-kot, below. See also sak"a-nt.
origin_explanation: in Job 4:11, where the Hebrew text has layiš 'lion', and the Septuagint has 'ant-lion'; perhaps also sa-kot, below. See also sak"a-nt.

meaning: 'lie down, be asleep, lie (with a woman)'; (sakba maslä 'abawihu 'he died', lit. 'he slept with his fathers'); 'askaba 'lay, lay down, lay (on a bed)'; sakäbi, act. part. of sakaba; sakub 'lying, lying down'; sakbat 'act of lying down'; maskab, maskabi, part. of 'askaba; maskab 'genitalia'; maskäb 'resting place to lie down, couch, bed'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. šakab 'lie down', Aram. šakeb, Syr. šakeb, Ug.Ph.Md. škb, Akk. šakapu. ETHIOPIC: Te. säkba 'lie down', Tna. säkäba.

phonetics: (instead of sakakäh of T.DWT 473, under waṭaši)
origin_explanation: see also (kähkoha), nakakäh.

meaning: name of month; from Heb. kislew through Gk. khaseleu.
origin_explanation: from Heb. kislew through Gk. khaseleu.

meaning: 'carry on the shoul-ders, carry a burden'; 'askama 'place on the shoulders'; tasakama, tasakma 'take upon one's shoul-ders'; sakam (K*) 'load'; sakum (K*) 'carried on the shoulders'; sakmat (K*) 'carrying (n) on the shoul-ders'; maskam, maskamt (pl. masäkäm) 'carrying pole, shaft, perch'; masäkäm (K*) 'means of carrying'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. šekem 'shoulder', that is, the part of the body on which one carries loads, Ug. tkm 'carry on the shoulders' (denominative from 'shoulder'). ETHIOPIC: Tna. täsäkäma, täsäkäma 'carry a load', Amh. täsäkäma, Gaf. täsäkäma.

meaning: 'sole of foot, forefoot, hoof, heel, footstep, socle, pedestal (of idol)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. säk"anä 'foot (of man or animal)', Amh. säk"anä, säk"anä, säk"anä, säk"ana, 'foot, hoof', Har.Gur. säxana; from Cushitic: suhänna-ta, Be. sukena, Bil. zag"anä (so also Cohen 1947, no. 265, and Brockelmann 1950:13-14). Brockelmann, ib. derives it from Be. sek 'walk, go', Sa. sig. See also Dolgopolsky 99.

meaning: 'mastic tree, (T.M) peach'; from Gk. skhinos, acc. skhinon 'mastic tree'. See also saginon.
origin_explanation: from Gk. skhinos, acc. skhinon 'mastic tree'. See also saginon.

meaning: 'that is crushed, that is broken into small pieces, that is crumbled up, that is grated';

'be drunk, be in-toxicated'; 'askara 'make drunk, intoxicate'; sakari (pl. sakart) 'drunkard, given to drink'; sakkär 'drunkard'; sakur 'drunk, drunken, inebriated'; sakär, sakir 'drunkenness, intoxication, intoxi-cating drink'; sakir 'drunkenness'; sakrat 'drunkenness, intoxication'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sakira 'be drunk', Soq. sekir, Heb. šakar, Aram.-Syr. šekar, Ug. škr, Akk. šakaru. Zim-mern 39, n. 2, is of the opinion that the verb is denominative from Akk. šikaru 'beer', Heb. šekar. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säkärä 'be drunk', Te. säkra, Amh. säkärä, Gur. säkärä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. sakar, Qua.Sa. sakar.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. saġara 'heat, kindle', Aram.-Syr. saġar 'become hot, heated, warm'. See also *saġ"ara II.

from Ar. sukkar of Sanskrit origin (Reinisch 1902:347); also in Amh.Gur.Har. sukkar, Te. sakkär, Tna. šakk"ar, šukk"ar; Amh. also šänk"ar 'sugar cane' (for the inserted n, cp. also Ar. [Hadr] sunkar). In Cushitic: Sa. šokar, Bil. sukar, Som. sonkor, sunkur.

meaning: 'come to an end, cease, decline, decrease, shrink, faint, recede, grow old (love), disappear, flee'; also transitive 'abandon, leave, sweep'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: von Soden 1139 compares Akk. šakāšu 'dry out, sweep'. Geez sakk"asa possibly comes from sk"-sk" and is to be compared with Heb. šakak 'abate, go down', Ar. saksaka 'become weak'. ETHIOPIC: also in Cushitic: Bil. šuqis 'be sick', Dem. šegus. See also sksk.
see sakk"asa.

meaning: 'kind of animal'; probably to be identified with sak"a-t, sak"a-nt, above.
origin_explanation: probably to be identified with sak"a-t, sak"a-nt, above.

meaning: 'street, lane, square, quarter, (T) crossroad, council, forum (held in a public square)'; sakota, for sak"ata (T.Y) "become a square, become a street" (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. suq 'market', Aram.-Syr. suqa (Fraen-kel 187), Heb. suq, Akk. suqu 'street', suqqaqu (suqa-qu) 'narrow street'. Brockelmann 1908:156 explains s-q > s-k (in Geez) by assimilation. Ar. sikka 'road, street', is considered by Noeldeke 1910:52 a Geez loanword. (So also A. Fischer 1953:55, with reserva-tions). For sikka in various Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1942:194. See also Kaufman 94. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. suq 'market', Te. soq is an Arabic loanword. The connection with Amh. sängo 'assembly' suggested by Beguinot 515 is unlikely. See also sakata II.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. säkätä 'pierce', Te. säkta 'throw a lance' (and possibly 'pierce with a lance').

meaning: 'encamp, lodge, rest'; is probably a denominative from sak"at 'quarter' (see above); for the meanings, cp. Amh. säffärä 'camp, lodge', and säfär 'section of a town'.
origin_explanation: is probably a denominative from sak"at 'quarter' (see above); for the meanings, cp. Amh. säffärä 'camp, lodge', and säfär 'section of a town'.
see sak"at.

meaning: 'church'; gives the impression of being a corrupt form in relation to Gk. katholikē (ekklesia) 'Catholic church'.
origin_explanation: gives the impression of being a corrupt form in relation to Gk. katholikē (ekklesia) 'Catholic church'.

meaning: 'cook on a griddle, put a kettle or a griddle on the fire'; tasaktata (M), passive; saktut (K*T) 'that is placed on the fire (kettle, griddle)'; saktat, säktat 'base, pedestal, foundation, (K) tripod on which a cooking pot rests, (T) peg, spike'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. säktäta 'put a kettle on the fire', Tna. säktätä; Amh.Tna. säktät 'base of a church door-post or column'. See also saktata II.
denominative from säktat 'base, pedestal', above.

meaning: 'conspire, bind one another by a solemn oath, plot'.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tə)säkkäwa (sky) 'be pierced, be wounded'. See also sakwa I.
is probably reconstructed from maskot 'window' (the root of which would then be skw). In case it is an original root, cp. Heb. sākā (sky) 'look', Aram. sākā 'look out'.

meaning: 'flee for refuge, take refuge, have recourse to (by prayers), appeal'; maskāy 'refuge, asylum, haven, support';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: M. Wagner, OLZ 49 (1954): 466 connects it with Ar. sakawa 'wander aimlessly'. However, considering the meaning of Ar. saka'a, it comes closer to G. sak'aya, below. ETHIOPIC: Te.Har. sākā 'flee', Gur. sāk'ä, Amh. säššā (for which see Cohen 1939:200, contrary to Praetorius 1879:132 who derives it from 'an-sosawa 'march'); also in Cushitic: Be. sak 'go, leave', Or. sakka 'flee' (contrary to Leslau 1979:541). Brockelmann 1950:14 considers it a Cushitic loanword. See also sakaya II.

meaning: 'lay an accusation, accuse, complain of, charge (with a crime), claim'; 'askaya (K*), causative; tasakaya 'be accused'; sakāyi 'accuser'; sakūy (K*) 'accused'; sakyat 'accusation, lawsuit'; maskāy (K.Gr) 'who accuses'; maskāy (K*) 'court' (that is, 'place of accusa-tion');
origin_explanation: while it is tempting to connect it with Ar. sakā (škw) 'accuse', the correspondence s:š is not nor-mal. It is rather to be identified with sakaya I, with the specialized meaning 'have recourse to a judge > accuse, complain of'. Note that Te. sāka 'accuse' is an Arabic loanword.

meaning: 'go astray, wan-der about aimlessly, rush about, (Y) be panic-stricken';
origin_explanation: tasak'aya, tasäk'aya, same; sak'ay (K*) 'go astray, wandering'; SEMITIC: Related to Heb. šāgā (šgy) 'stray', Aram. šāgā (Dillmann 383). For the root as related to Heb. šāgā, see Buhl 807. For Ar. sakawa, see sakaya above.
related to sakak, above.

meaning: (T.M.D 1402) 'arrow, (T*) devil's spear'; sakoza, for sak'aza (T), sakaza (MA) (denominative);
origin_explanation: from Gk. skhiza 'shaft, dart'.

meaning: (L.T.Sw. in D 1402) 'watercourse formed by erosion, riverbed'.

origin_explanation: see sayl.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. sila 'falcon, hawk'.
perhaps transcription of Latin sol.

meaning: 'take off, strip off, take away, remove, deprive, take spoils, plunder, despoil, ravish, confuse (the mind), bewilder'; tasalba, reflexive, passive; also 'be ravished, lose one's senses'; salābi (K*), act. part. of salaba; səlūb, pass. part. of salaba; also (Gr.409) 'undressed'; salabā 'spoils, which is stripped'; salbat, verbal noun of salaba;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. salaba 'take away, snatch', SAr. sļb 'draw water improperly', Šh. sļb 'take by force'. With alternance b:p in Heb. šālap 'draw out', Aram.-Syr. šālap, Md. šlp, Akk. šalāpu 'pull out'.
from Ar. ṣalaba. See also ṣalaba and salbib.
see salaba II.

meaning: (K*) 'walk backward, fall down on the back, stretch out'.

phonetics: saledā I, salidā, sāledā (pl. saledāt, salādəw)
meaning: (T) sāledā 'tablet, board, leaf of a book, thin metal plate, slate slab, (T.M) parchment, (T) smooth piece of wood';
origin_explanation: from Gk. selida (acc. of selis) 'column of writing'; also in Tna.Amh. sāleda 'tablet of a book, parch-ment'.

meaning: (K*) 'be lined up ready for battle'; salf 'battle formation';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. salafa 'go in front', sulfa 'a single line'. SAr. sļf 'façade (?). ETHIOPIC: Tna. säläfā 'be lined up ready for battle', sälfi 'row (of persons), order of battle, front, fore-part', Amh. säläfā 'be lined up ready for battle', Amh.Gur. sälf 'order of battle'; passed into Cushit-ic: Bil. säläf 'go in front', Kham. silaf.
transcription of Gk. Silōam, from Heb. mē haššiloḥ 'water-conduit'.
from Ar. salīḥa, originally 'skinned, stripped off'. also SAr. sļḥ 'cassia', Te.Tna. salḥay (Reinisch 1887:311). See also salikā.
SEMITIC: Ar. salaka 'travel, follow (a road)'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säläkā, sälakläkk bälä 'walk slowly, without making noise', Amh. sälläkä 'move at a rapid, even pace'.
from Ar. salīḥa, with G. k for ḥ in loanwords (see JSS 3[1958]:153, a doublet of salihot, above; also in Amh. sālik.
SEMITIC: Ar. salla 'be consumptive'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sälläla 'be paralyzed'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. sälläla 'baste'.

meaning: (K*) 'greet'; sallama (T.Y.M) 'make the sign of the cross, put in the form of a cross, crucify, suspend'; tasallama (Lt) 'take possession of, receive' (from Ar. tasallama); tasāläma 'greet one another, salute, venerate, make peace with one another, (Lt) bow down, submit'; 'astasāläma 'reconcile, make peace, give peace, make greet one another'; salām 'peace, salutation, safety'; (bä-salām 'in peace, safely'); (lä-lehu salām 'may peace be upon him!'); salāmā (K*) 'place of pacification, symbol of peace'; (wahaba salāma 'greet'); suläme 'concord, understanding, harmony, agreement, peace'; salāmhi, salāmyähi, see below; salāmwi 'peaceful, who likes peace, who seeks peace, peacemaking, friend'; salāmtā (Gr.161) 'collection of religious poetry sung on different occasions'; 'astasālāmi, act. part. of 'astasālāma; mastasālāmi, same;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. salima 'be intact, be safe', sallama 'greet, salute', SAr. h-s₂lm 'pacify', Soq. selim.

Heb. šālām 'be sound, keep peace', Aram.-Syr. šlāmā 'peace', Md. šlama, Ph.Ug. šlm, Akk. šalāmu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. salām bälä 'greet', salām 'peace', Te. salma 'greet', Amh. salām 'peace', täsälläma 'kiss (lit. 'greet') a sacred object'.
from salām 'peace', and -hi; for the ending -hi, see also qafähi.
perhaps a place-name.
from Gk. salamandra 'salamander'; also in Aram. salmandrā (Krauss 395).
from Gk. selinon 'celery'; also Amh. šälan 'anise', Te. šilan 'a plant', Tna. šälan. See also sibän II.
metathesis of sanodlis, from Ar. şandal (see sandal, below); for the final -s, cp. Md. sandlus, sandrus.
salip (T.M.MA) 'butter'; selp (T) 'butter'; salap (T.M) 'Virgin Mary', lit. 'the anointed'; perhaps to be identified with sarapa 'sip, celebrate Mass' (with alternance of liquids).
selpa (T.M) 'become a companion' (the form is strange; it looks as if it were a denomi- native from selp, below); selp (T), salep (MA) 'companion'; see also sarap.

meaning: 'joke, scoff at, deride, mock, ridicule, make fun of, make fun of one another'
origin_explanation: 'astasälaqa (K*), caus. of täsälaqa;

meaning: 'derision, cause of derision, ridicule, laughingstock, mockery, mocking (n), raillery, play'

origin_explanation: (Gr)

SEMITIC: Ar. salāqa 'vicious tongue, language', Ar. (Syria, Damascus) t-maqlas, t-maqlas 'mock' (denominative from a noun with m-; Denizeau 501). Margoliouth, JRAS 1939, p. 61 de- rives Ar. salāqa in Surah 33:19 from G. and translates it 'abuse with sharp t. Akk. tasliqtu 'eine KampfFrede', see von Soden 1339. The root represents a metathesis in relation to qilles 'jeer at'. For the identification of Aram.-Syr. qalles 'praise, sing praises of', PbH. qilles with Heb. qilles 'mock', see A. Bevan, in Noeldeke 1906:581-2. For the opposite meanings in cultic words, see brk and Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963).123. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)sälaqa 'mock', Amh. läqä; Gur. q'anäsä (for q'allāqä) has the s. Heb.Aram.-Syr. qls. The root passed into Qem. (tä)salāqä, Sa. salaq.

sallät 'laughingstock, jest, derision, mockery'. SEMITIC: Akk. šillatu 'blasphemy', contra von Soden 1236 who compares Akkadian with tions; related to Ar. salita, saluța 'be sharp, be foul-mouthed'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sällätä 'make fun of', Tna. sälätä 'recrimination, rebuke'. Coulbeaux-Schreiber 206 derive sälätä 'recrimination' from sallätä dry' > 'être cassant' (de parole ou caractère sälätä 'reproche, recrimination'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. sällätä 'dry out wither'. See also sal(l)ata I.
SEMITIC: Ar. salata 'cut', Akk. salātu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sälätä 'remove a plant from peel off', Amh. sällätä 'cut, shear', Te. sallata sälätä 'destroy', passed into Cushitic. Qem. sälät 'shear'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sällätä 'cover with a rough thatch'.
SEMITIC: perhaps Heb. selā, technical term of music or recitation. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sälti 'agreeable way of speaking, musical mode', Amh. sält 'mode of plainchant'.

meaning: 'be whole, be perfect, be finished, be consummated'
origin_explanation: saläta 'fulfill an obligation, finish, complete, make perfect, carry out successfully, make effective, pay back, receive in full, exhaust, eradicate'; vasaläta 'accomplish, make perfect, consum- mate, make efficient'; tasallata (Gr.163) 'be useful, serve'; tasalta, tasallata 'be finished, be accomplished, be consummated, be fulfilled, be made perfect, be efficient, prosper'; saläqi (K*), act. part. of saläta; sallät (Gr.163), act. part. of sallata; salluț 'perfect, accomplished, whole, entire, (Lt) accurate (report)'; salluța 'perfectly, accurately, absolutely'; solt (Lt) 'amount to be paid back'; sallätä 'soundness, completion, function, charge, usefulness, profit'; masläj (M.D 1429) 'efficient, (Gr.452) powerful'. SEMITIC: Noeldeke 1910:39, n.3 states that saläta "ist vielleicht der wahre Reflex von Heb. šälät 'domi- nate over'", and compares it, with reservations, with Ar. salit 'hard, strong'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sälätä 'be perfect', Te. sältä 'succeed, fit', 'aslata 'carry through, complete', Amh. sälätä 'be successful, finish (a task)', Har. sälätä 'be safe', Gur. sälätä 'get well, feel better'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. salat 'succeed'.
from Ar. salīt 'oil of sesame' in Yemen, elsewhere 'olive oil'; also in Te.Tna.Amh. sälīt 'sesame'; in Cushitic: Sa.Af. salīt, Som. salīd. For more details on Ar. salīt, see Landberg 1942:1965.
from Amh. sälatin.
D1395 records salwām without giving the meaning.
transcription of Gr. soam, from Heb. šoham; also in Te. šom 'glass bead, ornamental stone'.
SEMITIC: Ar. tūm 'garlic', Heb. šūm-im, Aram. šūm, Syr. tūmā, Akk. šūmu. See also somat, tummā.

meaning: 'hear, hear of, heed, give heed, obey, be obedient, listen, hearken, perceive, understand, bear witness, testify'
origin_explanation: 'asmä'a 'let be heard, make hear, bear witness, bring testimony, testify, attest, call a witness, cause to be a witness'; tasam'a, passive; also 'come to (one's ears), be spread (fame)'; tasam'a 'understand one another'; 'astasäm'a, causative of tasam'a; tasamämä'a (Lt) 'agree with one another' (note the reduplicative pattern of Amharic); samä'i (pl. samä'yān) 'auditor, hearing, lis- tening to, obedient'; for the pl. samä't, see below; sam'i 'obedience'; 'i-sami 'disobedience'; säm'u 'notable, one of authority, famous, illus- trious, notorious' (lit. 'one about whom one hears'); (sam'u zenā 'renowned'); säm'wät (pl. of säm'u) 'things heard, fame, reputation'; säm(ə)' 'rumor, news, testimony, evidence, witness, martyrdom'; säm'mā'e 'fame, glory'; säm'ät (pl. of samä'i) 'witness, testimony, mar- tyr'; säm'at, verbal noun of sam'a; also 'testimony, fame'; säm'atä tawähädət 'Unionists'; säm'atä (M) 'martyrdom'; 'asmä'i, masmä'i (Gr.165) 'who makes hear'

meaning: who makes hear, who makes testify'
'hearing, reach of hearing, sense of hearing, what is heard, obedience'
'base, support, prop, buttress; pre-Gospel reading that is considered the base for Gospel reading'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. sāmak 'support' (v.), Aram.-Syr. sa-mak, Md. smk 'support, rely on'; perhaps also Akk. samāku 'cover over'. Brockelmann 1928:480 also compares Ar. simāk, translating it 'support' by Ullendorf 1955:112, 63, is rendered 'go up, as-cend' in SabDic. 138.
from Ar. samak.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sämmälä 'rot' (given in MA).
perhaps a modern creation for sabaga, denomina-tive from Amh. sibago 'string', of Italian origin.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sämmämā 'be in a daze or a stupor, be half-awake'. Tna. sämäm bäla 'have the eyes closed' which indicates daze or stupor.
see sanbu.
seems to be a corrupt form of sahm 'arrow' (see above).
SEMITIC: Holma xiii, 83 compares it with Akk. sammāhu 'large intestine'; however, Akk. sammāhu is considered a Sumerian loanword. Reinisch 1887: 308 compares Bil. zanqi, sanqi 'liver'.

(denominative) SEMITIC: Ar. tamān 'eight', SAR. tmny, Soq. me-neh, Heb. šəmonə, Aram.-Syr. təmānə, Md. tmania, Ph. šmn, Ug. tmn, Akk. samānə, samānə. ETHIOPIC: Te. sman, Tna. šəmmantə, Amh.Arg. Gur. səmmənə, Gaf. səmmənə, Har. sūt < sumn-t. For 'eighty', cp. Te.Tna. Amh. sāmānya.

meaning: 'the eighth day (of a month), day, week'
'rest, lean, lean upon, prop up, sup-port, lay (the head), rely, make lean upon'
'lean on one another, join one another'

origin_explanation: Dillmann 334 identifies it with Ar. (al)tayman. Heb. tēmān 'Yemen' with alternance s:t. Praetorius 1879:153 (followed by Brockelmann 1908:77) de-rives it from *saymen 'turn toward right' (from ymn). Noeldeke 1910:82 correctly identifies it with the region of Sāmen, and explains the difference in the meanings 'south, north' by the fact that it is south of Axum, the ancient capital, and north of Gondar, the capital of the later period. For the same noun expressing different directions, see 'azeb dabub, and also Rhodokanakis, WZKM 25 (1911) 81 for the same feature in various Arabic dialects.

meaning: 'flourish, be fruitful, abound in fruit, grow abundantly'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. tamara 'bear fruit', SAR. tmr 'produce crops'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sämmärä 'be a bumper crop, be successful', Tna. sämärä.

meaning: = Amh. ənoqqo 'kind of bush whose fruit is used as an anthelmin-tic'
origin_explanation: see also sorart, sobart.
seems to be an interpretation of a proper name.

meaning: perhaps to be identified with Amh. sansa, same as samsāh
'pole that two men carry on their shoulders in order to transport a heavy object'
origin_explanation: see also samsām, below.

meaning: 'Holy Spirit', (MA) 'the place to which the Holy Spirit descended', 'devil, Satan'
origin_explanation: all these meanings seem to be in the same semantic sphere, but their original meaning is obscure. Note that Noeldeke, ZDMG 40 (1886), 736, does not separate Heb. šadday 'the name of God' from šēd 'evil spirit'. Akk. šēdu means 'eine Lebenskraft, ein Dämon' (von Soden 1208).

meaning: 'one who denies, one who is perfidious'
origin_explanation: refers to Pawlos Sāmasātīn 'Paul of Samosata', the heretic bishop of Antioch (206-265); see EMML, vol. 5, p. 172. See also sāmāṭi, below.
comes close to the structure of dimat, but the alternance d:s is not normal.
see sum, above.
could it be a variant of somat, samat 'fleshy tail of sheep, flank'?

meaning: 'loins, flank, waist, (T) fleshy tail of the sheep'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāmtī, šəmtī, šəmtī 'waist', Te. samāt 'flank, side', Amh. šənt, Har. šīntī 'filet'. Amharic čən 'thigh' is not derived from G. samāt by Praeto-rius 1879:95, as suggested by Appleyard 13; rather Praetorius refers it to an ancient Amharic form šənt, as given in Dillmann 341, from a Sāwāsw (G. samāt = Amh. šənt). The root also occurs in Cushit-ic: Bil. šīmit, šīmit. For mt > nt, mt > nt in Amh., cp. atəmt 'bone', from atəmt; antā 'bring' < amtā; for Gurage, see Leslau 1979:LXXXVI. See also samāt.
undoubtedly a misprint for sāmāṣi, above.

meaning: 'call, name, give a name, give a surname, address, (Gr) pray, invoke, appeal to'; (asmaya 'become renowned, become famous, be well-known'); (vasmaya sama 'acquire a good reputation'); tasamya 'be named, be called'; samāyi (K*) act. part. of samaya; samuy 'named, called, praised, honored, illustrious, one of renown, esteemed'; səm 'name, fame, reputation, renown'; asmāt (pl. of səm) 'secret names (of God, angels, demons used in magic against diseases, the evil eye, and so on), magic, witchcraft'; (asmāta šarāy 'magical names, witchcraft, magic'); samāwi (K*) 'pertaining to a name'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (•)ism 'name', SAr. s₂m, Šh. šum, Heb. šēm, Aram.-Syr. šəmā, Ug. Ph. šm, Akk. šumu. For this biradical root, see Noeldeke 1910:140-3. ETHIOPIC: Tna. səmāyā 'name, give a name', Te. sāma; Te.Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. səm 'name' (n.), Gaf. səm'ā. Cerulli 1938a:83 considers that this root is common to Semitic and Cushitic: Had. sum, Bil. sən, Be. sem, Wol. sum-ta.

SEMITIC: Ar. samāʾ, SAr. s₂my, Heb. šāmayim, Aram.-Syr. šəmayyā, Md. šumia, Ug. šm-m, Akk. šamû. For Soq. yitin, see Leslau 1938:78. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. səmāy, Arg. samay, Har. simi; passed into Cushitic: Kham. simay.

meaning: 'bake bread made of wheat' (called dafo in Amharic); denominative of samaz;
origin_explanation: (M 148 misunderstood the Amh. däffa [translation of sammaza] given by Tayyä. It is true that Amh. däffa means 'volto verso il basso, rovescio' [so M 148], but it also means 'bake the dafo-bread');

meaning: 'bread made of wheat' (Amh. dafo); masāmaz (T) 'oven, bakery';
origin_explanation: from Ar. samīd 'semolina'. For Ar. samīd in relation to other Semitic languages, see sandāle, below.

meaning: 'reed, tall grass, papyrus, rush, bush called əndod in Amharic (its leaves serve for washing clothes); sammaza (T.Y.M) 'uproot, turn over, (Sw. in D 1395) be worm-eaten';
origin_explanation: the verb is a denominative with a privative meaning for the privative meaning, cp. šərw 'root', and wa 'uproot'. ETHIOPIC: Te. səməza 'tree with edible fruit', səməza 'kind of bush', Amh. səmazza, səmuza 'shrub for making fences' (for which see D 1973:no.218); also in Cushitic: Qem. səma. see also səmazzāyəhu, below.

meaning: Amh. səmazza 'shrub used for making fences';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. səmazza, same.
perhaps rather sānsāl, a tree of the səmazza family and to be identified with səmazzāyəhu.
from Amh. san, sāhan, of Arabic (sahn) origin in Tna. sahan.

SEMITIC: Ar. sinn 'tooth', Heb. šēn, Aram. Syr. šen, Md. šine, Ug. šn-t, Akk. šinnu. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sənni, Arg.Har.Gur. sən, Gaf. Amh. sən (from Geez). Tigre sənnāt 'age of life', Tna. sənnāt 'coequal' is to be connected with 'tooth' (cp. also Ar. sinn 'tooth, age of a person is true that Šahri has two different roots: šnin 'tooth' and šən 'age', but Šh. šən could be borrowed from Arabic. See also sanana II, sənnām.

meaning: 'peace, unanimity, agreement, harmony; man of peace, peaceable, like-minded';
origin_explanation: the quadriradical verb sənw, with final w, in relation to the noun sən, would suggest a denominative. SEMITIC: Dillmann 377 (followed by Brockelmann 1928-773, and by Koehler 937) compares Heb. šāvan in šāvan 'be at ease', Syr. šayyen 'pacify'. The Geez root is related to Akk. šanāmu ('im Lauf') gleichkommen mit, gleichen' (von Soden 1161). Geez (ta)sanana suggested by von Soden IB., means 'quarrel' and is not to be compared with the Akkad-ian root. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)sana 'be friends', Tna. sənnit 'harmony', (tā)sanāyā 'agree with one another', Amh. (tā)sənnənā 'agree with one another', Gur. sənāt 'peace'.

meaning: 'be at peace, live in peace, be of one mind, be in harmony with one another, agree, make an agreement, coincide, concur, be in accord, be unanimous, be on friendly terms, enter into a confederation, be adapted to'; astasanāwa, causative; sanəw 'peace-giving, equal, agreeing, who is in agreement, consenting'; sənəwē 'harmony, agreement, unanimity'; tasanāwi, act. part. of tasanāwa; tasanāwəwo (t) 'unanimous agreement, consent, harmony, alliance, concord, cohesion, communion'; tasanāwəwot 'disagreement'; astasanāwəwi, part. of tasanāwa; san, see above;
origin_explanation: the quadriradical verb sənw, with final w, in relation to the noun sən, would suggest a denominative. SEMITIC: Dillmann 377 (followed by Brockelmann 1928-773, and by Koehler 937) compares Heb. šāvan in šāvan 'be at ease', Syr. šayyen 'pacify'. The Geez root is related to Akk. šanāmu ('im Lauf') gleichkommen mit, gleichen' (von Soden 1161). Geez (ta)sanana suggested by von Soden IB., means 'quarrel' and is not to be compared with the Akkad-ian root. ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)sana 'be friends', Tna. sənnit 'harmony', (tā)sanāyā 'agree with one another', Amh. (tā)sənnənā 'agree with one another', Gur. sənāt 'peace'.
the derivation from s²l 'ask' (with inserted n) suggested by Dillmann 377 is unlikely.
from s²y (see šaraya), enlarged by n.
ETHIOPIC: Te. sənbu, səmbur, Tna. sanbu, səmbur, samba, sambo, Amh. samba; from Cushitic: Bil. sənbi, Kham. səbba, Or. somba, Qab. šəmbu; for the other Cushitic languages, see Dolgopolsky 94.

meaning: 'ear of grain, grain that is still green but is ripe enough to eat';
origin_explanation: from Ar. sunbul, also Amh. sänbäl. See also (sabla), sabl.
from Ar. sunbul 'plant of sweet odor, spikenard'; also Tna. sənbu, Amh. sənbal, sənbur 'aromatic plant, spikenard'.
see also sobel.
from Ar. as-sunbula, originally 'ear of corn' (see Kunitzsch 1961:no.275). also Amh. sänbula.
from Ar. sanawbar; also Te. sänobär, Amh. sänobar.

meaning: 'Sabbath, Sunday, week'; Sabbath: qadāmit sanbat, qadāmi sanbat, qadāmit, lit. 'first Sabbath'; sanbata 'ayhud, lit. 'the Sabbath of the Jews'; Sunday: 'əhud sanbat, sanbata 'əhud, lit. 'first Sabbath'; sanbata krəstiyān, lit. 'the Sabbath of the Christians'; (sanbata mədr 'a year of rest [for the land]'); (ba'āla sanbat 'Feast of Weeks, Pentecost'); (sanbata mahāṣəwa fāsika 'Easter eve');
'Sabbatical'; (T.Y.K.M) 'spend a week, take a rest, celebrate' (denominative); asanbatata, causative 'also 'keep (observe) the Sabbath'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. sänbät 'Saturday, Sab- bath', Te. sämbät, Arg. sämbäb; from Heb. šabbāt, Syr. šabbātā, with inserted n (see also Noeldeke 1910:37). The denominative 'spend a week, spend a certain time' also occurs in other Ethiopian lan- guages: Tna. sänbätä, Te. sänbätä, Amh. sänabbätä, Gur. sänabbätä. For 'take a rest', cp. Heb. šābāt, Ar. sabata. The root passed into Cushitic: Qua. sänbät, Sa. sambat, Kham. sinbit. On the Sabbath in Ethiopia, see E. Hammerschmidt, Stellung und Be- deutung des Sabbats in Äthiopien (1963).

meaning: 'banner, standard, flag, (M) prayer stick'; sandaqa (T.Y) 'hoist a flag' (denominative)
origin_explanation: from Amh.Tna. sändäq, from Ar. sanqaq (of Turkish-Persian) origin.
from Gk. synodikon, same; also Amh. sinodiqon. See also sinodos.
from Persian through Ar. sindarūs; on the Indian origin, see Littmann 1926:416; also in Te. se 'white incense', Amh. sändäros 'white juniper resin'.

meaning: (Y.M) 'throw, forget'; 'throw down > *abandon > forget'; cp. Tna. sändadä 'throw stones'.
'synod, ecclesi- astical assembly, synodical canons'; (maṣḥafa senodos, religious work containing the canons of the first church councils; for a bibliography, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983: 302).
origin_explanation: from Gk. synodos 'synod'; also in Tna.Amh. sino- dos, Chr.-Ar. sinūdus.
from Ar. sandal (from Persian, of Sanskrit origin; see Littmann 1926:416); also Te.Amh.Gur. sändal. For Amh. sändal, see Strelycn 1973:no.217. See also salondis.
from Ar. sindiyān 'evergreen oak'.

meaning: 'fine flour, fine wheaten flour'; would SAr. sḍl 'measure of fine flour', Amh. sändəle 'wheat' be of the same origin?
origin_explanation: Praetorius 1879:78 connects Amh. sändəle with G. šärnäy. B. Landsberger, OLZ 25 (1922). 343 derives G. sändāle from Gk. semidalis 'fine flour'. It is inter- esting to note that this root comes close to Ar. samīd, samīd < Syr. samīd < Akk. samīdu. For an attempt at connecting Ug. smd with Akk. samīdu, see Caquot 476, note n.

probably from Amh. sänāfil, from Ar. sarāwīl, sirwāl, of Persian origin (Armbruster 703; Litt- mann-Höfner 221); also in Tna. Te. sänāfil in Cushitic: Qab. sälänfilu. For Ar. sirwāl, see Dozy 1845:203-9, Landberg 1942:1928.

meaning: (Lt) sandun, sondun 'fine linen, fine garment, linen cloth, gown'
origin_explanation: from Gk. sindōn 'garment of linen'; also in Ar. sundus 'silk brocade', Syr. sedūnā 'piece of cloth', for which see Fraenkel 48. On the connection between Gk. sindōn and Akk. saddinu 'piece of cloth', see Kaufman 94, n.324.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'work, make, (K*) put a ring in the nose, make solid, make strong'; sang"ag" 'firm, massive, solid'; sang"ag" (pl. sandäg"ag") 'hook for fastening, clasp, ring passed through the nose, (T) bolt for a door'.
origin_explanation: perhaps rather sang"ag"a I 'make, work' sang"ag"a II 'put a ring in the nose', unless in translation given by T is too vague. For 'put a ring in the nose', cp. Amh. sänag 'ring put in the nose', sännägä 'put a ring in the nose'. Tigrinya has säng"ag"a 'throw a stick to hit'.

meaning: (T.M) 'make an incision in the flesh with a sharp instrument in order to cup blood'
origin_explanation: it is possible that it is a misprint in relation to ሠንቀሐ sanhota with a spelling variant ḥ:ḥ and ኀ:ሐ for ኀ + ሐ. See also sanhoqa.

meaning: (yasnäḥ) 'be bald, be supple (hide, leather)'; sənḥu 'bald'; sənḥat 'baldness of the forehead'; sənḥ (T) 'whitish rash on the scalp'.
(T.M), sanhoṭa (MA) 'make an incision in the flesh with a sharp instrument in order to cup blood'
origin_explanation: see also sanhoqa.
see also sankoris.
from Gk. synkhōrēson imper. sg. aorist of synkhōrein 'forgive', or synkhōrēsōn 'one who will for- give'. As for G. sankoris, it comes close to Gk. synkhoreis 'you forgive'. See also sankora.

meaning: 'synaxarium, martyrologium, collection of legends of the saints'
origin_explanation: from Gk. synaxarion, same meaning, through Chr.- Ar. sinkisār; also Tna.Amh. sankasar. For a bibliog- raphy on the sankasār, see Hammerschmidt-Six 1983:293.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'bake bread'; MA 154 wrongly has sahakota for sankota, sankata.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sänkätä 'bake bread', Te. sänkätä.
probably from Ar. sanām 'hump'; also in Te. salam (with alternance of liquids) passed into Cushitic: Bil. selam.

meaning: (T) 'one who has buck-teeth' from sänn 'tooth' with the Amharic suffix -am.
(yəsnən) (K*) 'sharpen'; tasannana (K*) 'be sharpened'; sanāni (K*) 'one who sharpens'; sənun (K*) 'sharp'.
origin_explanation: possibly a denominative from sänn 'tooth'; SEMITIC: Ar. sanna 'sharpen', Heb. šānan, Aram.- Syr. šəninā 'sharp, sharp-tongued'. See also sanana II.

meaning: (K*) 'grow teeth' (denominative from sänn 'tooth'); sanana, sänana (D.K*) 'fight with the teeth, fight, plead'; tasannana (K*) 'have (large) teeth, be bitten'; tasannana, tasänana 'quarrel, dispute, plead, strive, contend with, enter into litigation with'; 'astasannana, 'astasänana, causative of tasanna- na, tasänana; sanāni (K*) 'who grows teeth, who bites'; sənun (K*) 'who grows teeth'; tasnān 'quarrel, dispute, contradiction, discord, feud, controversy, lawsuit'; tasnun (Gr.453) 'litigious' (from tasnān); mastasānān (K*), part. of tasänana.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sänna 'bite, oppose to', 'asanna 'grow teeth', Šh. esnin; von Soden 1161 compares Akk. sanānu (im Lauf) gleichkommen mit, gleichen' with G. tasannana, but see snw. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)sänänä 'quarrel', Arg. (as)san- na 'dispute'. Gur. (tä)sänänä (where s is palatalized into š due to the following vowel e); perhaps also Har. sinān 'language', sinān āša 'quarrel', (a)sēnāna 'speak, talk'.
to be identified with tasnān 'quarrel' (see sanana II).
from Gk. sināpi 'mustard'; also Tna.Amh. sänäfä, sənäpa (from Oromo).
from Gk. sappheiros 'sapphire'; see sofor, above.

meaning: 'play a musical instrument (harp, flute)'; causative; 'pass. of sanqawa'; 'musician, who plays a musical instrument'; 'sound of stringed instruments, playing a musical instrument'; 'one-stringed fiddle' (see Powne 40-4); 'one who plays a musical instrument, musician';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sänqawa 'play an instrument', Te. sänqa 'play on the harp', Amh. sänqaqa. For G. masanqo, cp. Te.Tna.Amh. mäsanqo, mäsanqo. Dillmann 369 derives the root from nqw 'produce a sound' with the agglutinated morpheme s of the causative. The root is perhaps common with Cushitic: Qem. sanko 'lyre', Rabin, EI 9 (1969). 154 considers sanqawa a denominative from masanqo which he derives, with reservations, from Gk. mousikē, mousika. The root passed into Cushitic: Qua. mäsanqō, Kham. mizinq'a.
From Amh. sänsäl.

meaning: 'chain, link'; 'chain, link, bind together'; 'passive'; 'be linked together, be chained together, be interlaced'; 'chain'; 'act. part. of sansala'; 'chained, linked'; 'linking (n) together';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. silsila 'chain' (on slsl, snsl in various Arabic dialects, see Landberg 1942:1964). SAr. s₂lslt (W. Miller 1983:283), Soq. silsileh, Hars. senselēt, Heb. sarsəra, PBH sālsəlā, Aram. tūšeltā, šəšaltā, Syr. šišaltā, Md. šultalta, Ug. šrrt, Akk. šeršerra. ETHIOPIC: Noeldekc 1910:42 considers G. sansal a Heb.-Aram. loanword; also in Tna.Amh. sänsälät, Te. sänsälät, Har. sänsälät, sinsälät; passed into Cushitic: Bil. sänsälä, Be. sinsil, sinsil.
one wonders whether this is the correct meaning since sansariq'at is the plural of sansariq'.
see also serenisros.

meaning: 'shine, sparkle, scrape, polish, smooth out, beautify, decorate'; 'passive'; 'act. part. of sansa'; 'that shines'; 'something smoothed or shaped, decoration'; 'brightness, brilliancy, splendor'; 'means of shining, scraping, polishing, beautifying';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sännätä 'do something attractively, make something pretty'. See also sansata.

meaning: 'find out something in a legal case by examining witnesses, bring to an agreement (by reconciling conflicting testimony), make two copies agree by clearing up the difficulties'; 'originally, 'make brilliant, make bright, clear up'';
origin_explanation: denominative from sansat; see sansa, above.
see sänay.
From Amh. santi.
From Amh. säntära 'pocket of a sling or catapult'.
From Coptic sonte, sante (W. Müller). On santaw in magical texts, see Strelcyn 1955:429.
from Ar. šatranğ, šitranğ, from an Indian language through Persian (Littmann 1926:416); also in Tna. säntäräğ, Amh. säntäräg.

meaning: 'the next day, the day after'; 'the next day'; 'on the next day'; 'the second day of the week or month, two days'; 'two days'; 'two days'; 'make two' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: from the root 'two'; Ar. 'itnāni 'two', tāni 'second', SAr. tny 'two', Mh. tru, Heb. šənayim, Aram. tren, Md. trin, Ph. šn-m, Ug. tn, Akk. šina; note Egyptian Ar. tāni yōm 'the next day'. ETHIOPIC: Te. säno 'Monday', Tna. sänuy, Amh. sänita 'the next day, the day after' (from Geez); passed into Cushitic: Bil. sänä 'Monday'. For the root for 'two' used in the various Ethiopian languages, see käl'e.
probably a misprint for 'ap, or the other way around.
sopa, sopu; see swp.
Amh. əmbə'əččo 'kind of blind-rat'. See also sappalaksəs, below.
From Gk. spalaks 'blind-rat'. See also sappalaksəs, below.
see also sappalaksəs.
From Gk. speira 'military unit'.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. säqq, same (from Geez). KBT 245 in describing this noun as meaning 'heaven' seems to consider it to be like a wide tent and to identify it with säqq 'sackcloth', possibly serving for making a tent.
see swq.
from Ar. sūq; also in Amh. suq, Te. soq, Tna. suq, suq. See also Landsberger, in Baumgartner 1967:185.

meaning: 'cut a vein in the forehead to bleed or cup a person, place a barrier across a road';
'ignite, light, set on fire, fire (a gun)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. taqqaha 'make a fire burn lightly, burn'.

meaning: 'suspend, hang, hang up, crucify'; 'causative'; 'passive; also 'be in suspense, depend, adhere, cling to, (Lt) be devoted'; 'act. part. of saqala'; 'hung up, hung upon, hanging, suspended, crucified, dependent, who clings to, who climbs'; 'hanging (n), crucifixion'; '(ba'äla saqalat 'Good Friday')'; '(subä'e saqalat 'the week of the crucifixion, Holy Week')'; 'cross'; '(ba'äla masqal 'Feast of the Finding of the True Cross'; it is celebrated on the 16th and 17th of Maskaram)'; 'pertaining to the cross';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. siqlhel 'high' (from 'hang up > place in a high position'), Ar. taqala 'lift to test the weight' (even though the basic meaning is 'weigh'), Akk. šaqālu, šaqallulu 'hang'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säqälä 'hang', Te. säqäla, Amh. säqälä, Har. säqäla, Gur. säqälä; passed into Cushitic: Kham. siql. See also msql, and saqala II.

SEMITIC: Ar. taqula 'be heavy', taqala 'weigh', Heb. šāqal 'weigh', Aram.-Syr. taqal, Anc.Aram.Ph. šql, Akk. šaqālu; perhaps to be identified with saqala I, and cp. Akk. šaqālu 'hang, weigh' from a basic meaning 'weigh by lifting (hanging)'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sāqqalā 'weigh'. See also saql, below.
saql, saql transcribes Heb. šeqel, while saqlos tran-scribes Gk. siklos, siglos. For Heb. šeqel, cp. Aram. tiqlā, Ug. tql, from Akk. šiqlu (von Soden 1248), from saqala 'weigh', above.

meaning: 'oblong house or tent, royal camp, shed, tabernacle, (Gr.409) large rectangular house'; (saqalā samāy 'arch, vault of sky').
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sāqālā 'large rectangular house', Tna. sāqāla, Te. saqlo, Gur. sāqala; also in Cushitic: Or. sāqāla.
ETHIOPIC: Te. sqm, in sāqāqama 'pluck'.
From Gk. sykaminos 'mulberry tree'. See also saqam-ros.
From Gk. sykəmoros 'sycamore' (Hommel 1887:94). See also seqemon.
See also sakinon.
From Gk. skopiān (acc. sg. of skopiā) 'lookout place'.

meaning: *saq*aq*a (K*) tasaq*aq*a 'covet someone's goods, (Gr.410) steal, misappropriate'; *asq*aq*a (K*), *astasq*aq*a, causative of ta-saq*aq*a.
