origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sāqa (šwq) 'fill with longing', in tasūqā 'desire, impulse, urge' (Dillmann 353), Aram. šūqā 'eager desire'. Driver, JTS 35 (1934) 387 compares Heb. šōqeq (root šqq) 'be yearn' (in Prov. 28:15). ETHIOPIC: Te. šawwāqa 'desire', from Arab.

meaning: 'perforate, pierce through, bore, sting, cleave, dig through'; *asq*ara (K*), causative; tasaq*ara, passive; saq*āri (K*Gr.167), act. part. of saq*ara; saq*ur 'full of holes, perforated, pierced, vated, (Lt) hole'; saq*rat 'opening, aperture, hole, cleft, hole, eye (of a needle)'; masqar, masqar 'ship, Noah's ark (that is, 'hollowed out'), (T*) window, (Sw.in D 352) slab on which the Ten Commandments inscribed'; masq*ar (K*) 'instrument for piercing, boring'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. tqr 'dig a cistern' (Müller 1983:284), šaqar 'morning' (from 'pierce', cp. boqer 'morning', from bqr 'pierce', Ar. faqr) šaqāru 'pierce through'; related to Ar. taqara 'break, breach a wall'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāq*arā, šānāq*arā 'perforate', šānāq*arā 'stir up, grub up the earth'. Amh. sāq*arā. See also saqārār.

meaning: 'incision, veil through which the wearer may see out but outsiders may not see in'; M. 151 wrongly translates aynä raqab given by T(ayyä) as 'the dove's eyes', which is a literal translation of aynä raqab meaning 'veil'; to be identified with saq*ara I 'pierce', suggesting the holes in the veil.
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1399 (under habr) from Ar. hibrə-s-sira, but sira does not seem to have any meaning that would go with habr 'color'; cp. rather Ar. sirā'a (Kazimirski) 'kind of striped cloth'. Or could it be identified with šeraya I 'dye'?
perhaps for šaqšāqa 'be intertwined' > 'be in a mess'.
seems to be a compound noun.
see sətuqāte, below.

meaning: saqaya 'irri-gate, water'; tasaqaya 'be watered'; saqāyi, act. part. of saqaya; saquy 'irrigated'; saqayā (Lt) 'canal'; saqayat 'irrigation'; masqāy 'water reservoir'; masqe, masqet (pl. masqāy) 'jug, vessel for oil'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. saqā (sqy) 'give to drink, irrigate, water', SAr. sqy, Šh. šuqi, Heb. šāqā 'give to water', Aram. šəqā 'give to drink', Syr. šaqqi 'to water', Md. šqa (af'el) 'give to drink', Ug. šqy 'drink', Akk. šaqû 'to water'. See also sawāqi.
in habra sirā 'chess' derived, with reservations, by
SEMITIC: Ar. tawr 'bull, ox', SAr. twr, Heb. šōr, Aram. tōrā, Syr. tawrā, Md. taura, Ug. tr, Akk. šūru. See also sawr.
it seems to be an incomplete entry.
probably from Amh. sora 'colored cloth'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qua.Aw.Qem. sar 'be red'.

meaning: Amh. lämš awāṭṭa 'be a leper', lit. 'bring out leprosy'.
origin_explanation: see sar*ama, soryama.

meaning: (M) = Amh. lämš awāṭṭa 'skin an animal and use its skin for making a lämš-coat'.
origin_explanation: the Amharic translation given by Y should read lämš awāṭṭa, for which see sor*ema, above.

meaning: 'flood' (v.); *asraba (T*) 'cause to stream in torrents'; sarb (T) 'big jar of water'; *asrāb (pl. of *sarab, sarb) ' torrents, showers (of rain), cataracts, waterfalls, canals, flood gates, sluices, locks'; *asrābāt (Lt) 'canals'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sariba 'flow', sarab 'canal, flowing water'; perhaps also Ar. zariba 'flow', zirb 'water-course'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sārābā 'begin to rain', Amh. asrab 'cataract, flood' (from Geez).
(See Strelcyn 1973:no. 76).

meaning: Amh. amarit 'the three cords of the bägäna-musical instrument, female singer'.

origin_explanation: From Amh. sirdo 'kind of grass'; also Te. sirddt, Tna. sirdo 'tough grass that is difficult to eradicate'; also in Cushitic: Be. särde, Qem. särdo.
from Ar. sirdib, pl. of sirdāb, same.

phonetics: sardino, sardayon (K*) 'Sardian stone, precious stone'; tasardana (Lt) 'be adorned'
origin_explanation: from Gk. sardion 'Sardian stone'; also in Amh. särdayon, särdayos, Te. särdayos.

phonetics: sardonaks (T) sardonakyos (OT 574, T) sardonaks 'sar-donyx, precious stone'
origin_explanation: from Gk. sardonyks, same; also in Amh. särdä-nayos, Te. särdonaks. See also radonaks.

phonetics: sarafa (yasraf) (T.Y.M) 'perforate'; masraf (T.M) 'instrument for boring'; masrafi, in masrafe 'anf (T.M) 'instrument to perforate the nostrils'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sarafa 'gnaw leaves or bark of a tree'; possibly a denominative from surfa 'kind of worm'. ETHIOPIC: Te. särfa 'break, cut down, become gap-toothed', Tna. särräfa 'chip (a utensil), lose the teeth', Amh. särräfa 'chip, notch', Gur. särräfa 'break off a piece', Arg. särräfa, Har. säräfa 'chip, break, lose the teeth'; passed into Cushitic: Al. säräffo, Aw. säräf-.
from Amh. särf.
from Heb. śərāpīm through Gk. seraphim; also in Amh. Tna. suräfel.
SEMITIC: Ar. sarīga 'sack made of palm leaves', Heb. śrg (pu'al) 'be intertwined', PBh. śrg 'intertwine', Aram.Syr. sarag, Md. srg, srg. For its connection with śgr, see śagara II, below.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. särragi 'liquid that percolates or seeps'.

phonetics: saragallä (K.T) saragallä 'wagon, chariot, cart, carriage, wheel, (K*) be saragalläwi 'chariot driver, pertaining to a char-iot'
origin_explanation: from late Gk. saragaron 'kind of wagon'; Aram. sargalā (see Růžička 22-3; Littmann 1926:417); in Ethiopic: Tna.Amh. särägälla, Qem. särägälla 'beam'.

SEMITIC: Ar. sarraga 'embellish', Akk. šergu-m 'be decorated'. Dillmann 348 (following Hupfeld) compares Aram.-Syr. sarag 'plait, entwine'. ETHIOPIC: Amh.Gur.Gaf. särg 'wedding' (Praetorius 1879:41), Amh. särrägä 'give a wedding feast'. The connection with Tna. sargam(m)ä 'a woman's hairdo' suggested by Wajnberg 1935:68 is unlikely. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. särgä 'adornment', sirg 'i 'bride', Qua. sergü 'bridegroom'.

phonetics: sarha I (yasräḥ) (T.Y) sarha (W.C) sarha 'toil, labor to the point of exhaus-tion, drudge, weary oneself, be fatigued, troubled, be anxious; (Amharism) fabricate, make, set (the hair), till (the land)'; 'asraha 'weary, wear out, tire, exhaust, torment, oppress, afflict, push on, bring trouble, trouble, (Lt) deaden, smother'; sarähi, act. part. of sarha; sarh 'toil'; saruḥ 'tired, one who labors, oppressed'; saräḥ, (T) saräḥ 'labor, fatigue, exhaustion, trouble, hard work, sorrow, bother, affliction'; masräḥ, act. part. of 'asraha
meaning: toil, labor to the point of exhaustion, drudge, weary oneself, be fatigued, troubled, be anxious; (Amharism) fabricate, make, set (the hair), till (the land)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. razaha 'become tired, become exhausted', Soq. razah, suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 62 (1908), 753, is unlikely though the meaning is the same. ETHIOPIC: Te. särha 'make, manufacture', Te. särhe 'make, work', Amh. särra; passed into Cushitic: Is. seräḥ 'work', Qua. sär.

phonetics: sarha II (Lt) 'dismiss, dismiss a congrega-tion at the end of Mass'; sarhot 'dismissal'
meaning: dismiss, dismiss a congregation at the end of Mass
origin_explanation: From Chr.-Ar. saraha 'dismiss someone with a blessing at the end of service'.
gives the impression of a proper noun becoming a common noun.

phonetics: sark (K) sark 'evening, twilight'; ('abiyy sark 'vespers'); (za-sark 'vespers'); (salota sark 'vespers'); 'asraka, (Y) 'asraka 'do in the evening, spend the evening' (denominative)
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Syr. šarek 'be left, be reduced to', Ar. sarika 'become weak' suggested by Brockelmann 1928:808 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Amh. särk 'evening', särräkä 'become evening' (from Geez).

phonetics: seraka (T.Y.MA), seraka (M) 'be intelligent, be wise, (Y) write'
origin_explanation: denominative from Sirak, to whom the Book of Sirach (Sirak), a book of wisdom, is attributed; cp. also Amh. sirak 'wise', sarraka 'be wise' (KBT). For a similar development, cp. Salomon (T) 'wise', referring to King Solomon.

phonetics: siräk, siräk 'line, column, table, division of a table, segment, section, para-graph, (Gr.166) table of concordance, (K*) lace, shoelace, strap of sandal'; saraka (K*) 'trace a line, plait, sew straps of leather' (denominative)
meaning: line, column, table, division of a table, segment, section, paragraph
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sirāk 'strip of herbage, line, strap of sandals', Heb. śərōk 'shoelace'. For 'line', cp. Syr. sargā 'line'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sirak 'blank space at the top of the first page of a book', Te. Amh. säräk 'strap of sandals', Tna. sərāk; passed into Cushitic: Bil. zerka. There is the possibility of separating the root into siräk I 'line', and siräk II 'shoelace'. In this case siräk II 'shoelace' would come from the root srk 'entangle'. Heb. säräk, Aram. sərāk, and probably also Ar. 'asrākā 'tie something closely to something else'.
OT 574 has sarmät, but the text of 1 Chr. 11:15 has sarmän.
note that -hi occurs elsewhere, apparently as a suffix.

phonetics: sarnon, siranon, seranon, serinon, sarenes, seri-nis, serenis, sernes 'siren, ostrich, jackal'
origin_explanation: from Gk. seirēn, pl. seirēnes, gen. pl. seirēnōn 'siren, sea nymph, part woman, part bird'; also in Aram. sirūnīt, Syr. sirin. The Hebrew text has either 'ostrich' or 'jackal'; see M. Cohen, "Sur la définition et le nom des sirènes", in Donum nataliciuт Schrijnen (1929), pp. 228-239; "Sur les sirènes en Abyssinie" (with Roger Chambard), RHR 98 (1928):94-107.
ETHIOPIC: comes close to Tna. särnih 'quail'. See also senesros, and perhaps in connection with sar-non.
see also selpa.
see sarapa I.

meaning: 'celebrate Mass, bless an object, sip' (the sipping of the blessed wine being a part of the Mass);
origin_explanation: a variant of šaraba, below. See also salapa I, sarapa, and sarapa II.
serpu (MA) 'heaven' (originally 'place of rest'); perhaps to be connected with sarapa I 'celebrate Mass > 'celebrate a holiday > *not work > take a rest'. See also sahapa.

'asraqa, causative; also 'falsify'; tasaraqa, pass. of saraqa; tasaraqa 'steal from one another'; 'astasaraqa, 'astasaraqa 'let be stolen, make steal from one another'; saraqi (pl. saraqt) 'thief, robber'; saruq 'stolen, robbed, taken away secretly'; sarq, sarq 'theft, stolen object, robbery'; sariqo (he) secretly, furtively'; sorqat (K*) 'act of stealing, robbing'. SEMITIC: Ar. saraqa 'steal', SAr. s.r.q, Sh. šeroq, Syr. sarreq 'lay waste, deprive', Md. sra 'empty' (v). Akk. šarāqu. Brockelmann 1908:526, n. 2, and 1928:501 explains the root by a secondary saf'al, šaf'al from a root ryq 'be empty'. This may be valid for Syriac, but not for Geez or Arabic where a root ryq does not exist, unless one considers ryq a Proto-Semitic root that has disappeared in various Semitic languages. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säräqa 'steal', Te. särqa, Amh. särräqa, Arg. särräqa, Gur. säräqa.

meaning: 'commemoration of a dead person by a religious service and banquet' (corresponding to täzkar)
origin_explanation: (corresponding to täzkar)

meaning: 'fly, forth, flee, leap up in the air, leap upon, rush upon, spring forth, assault, cover (of male animal), roam'
origin_explanation: 'asraara, causative; also 'stir up, make drive away'; tasarra (K*) 'be covered (female animal) attacked'; sarari, act. part. of sarara; also 'winged creature'; sarur 'flying, that is in flight, that flies'; sarrat, verbal noun of sarara; also 'flight (of a bird)'. SEMITIC: Akk. šarāru 'sway, vacillate' (for which see von Soden 1952:136). For Ug. m-srr 'bird' (from srr 'fly'), see Caquot 514; for Aram. srsr 'fly', see Driver 1935:56. The root is related to Ar. s.r.r (swr) 'leap, mount' (Armbruster 499). Praetorius, ZDMG 62 (1908).749-50 connects it with farra 'flee', with the passage of f to t > s. Rabin, HS 90 connects it with Ar. sarra 'rejoice'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. särärä 'mount', Te. särra 'jump', Amh.Gur. särrärä 'jump, mount, climb, copulate (male animal)', Arg. särrärä, Har. särärä; passed into Cushitic: Be. serär.
transcription of Gk. sarra, from Heb. šōri.
since the line above (in T. p. 10, supplement) has somart 'kind of bush whose fruit is used as an anthelmintic', it is possible that sorart is a misprint in relation to somart; see also sobart.
could it be a secondary form of sarnon, sarenes?

masarsar (Lt) 'saw' (n.); SEMITIC: Ar. sarsara 'split' (Armbruster 503). ETHIOPIC: Tna. särsärä 'make a hole with a hard object', Amh. säršärä, šäršärä 'bore'. Har. sirsirä 'remove the bone from the meat', Gur. säršärä 'bore a hole through'; passed into Cushitic: Som. sar 'incision', Aw. särsär-ä-x'a 'make a make an incision', Qem. särsär 'make a hole'.
transcription of Gk. serseroth, from Heb. šaršōr 'chains'.

meaning: 'cover of a cooking pot, means of hiding'

origin_explanation: is perhaps a misprint for semata, semata (see above), unless it is to be compared to Tna. šärätä 'the market is under way, the market starts'.
see sarara.
see (saraya), säryat.
from Ar. saratān, originally 'crayfish' (from Syriac according to Brockelmann 1928:499); also in Amh. šärtan.
perhaps for šarwe and derived from šarrawa 'root out' (see šarw); sarwe (šarwe) would then be 'matter that is uprooted > dirt, filth'.

meaning: 'break asunder a rod, (K*) be dislocated, be sprained'
origin_explanation: särwäš, (T) särwäš 'splinter, chip, broken staff of reed, wedge, (T) shoot, bud, gloss (= wedge, insertion)'; SEMITIC: The root seems to be a quadriradical augmented by w from srs (cp. Ar. saras 'incision'); perhaps related to šaşara 'split' (see below). Dillmann 346 derives it, with reservations, from a root rəşa 'Heb. rāṣaṣ 'crush', Ar. radda. Nyberg 213 analyzes it as ša-rawāš 'das des Abschneidens, Abgeschnittenes' > *šarwāš > särwäš. Rundgren, OS 10 (1961). 128 derives särwäš from *šurwāš < šur-bāš (a crossing between šərāb 'splinter' and šəşär) > šərbāš > särwäš. He also considers, with reserva-tions, the possibility of *šəšäwär (pl. of šəşşär 'splinter') > *šärwäš > sg. särwäš. ETHIOPIC: Amh. särwaš 'splinter, wedge, parentheti-cal remark' (from Geez), sərwətt alä 'be sprained'.
see sarwe I.

meaning: 'pardon, absolve, expiate, forgive, excuse, (Lt) comply, accede'
origin_explanation: tasarya, passive; also 'be atoned'; tasäraya (K*) 'forgive one another'; 'asraya (K), 'astasraya 'cause to forgive, intercede, make atonement, be forgiving, ask for forgiveness, obtain remission of sins'; 'astasäraya 'pardon completely'; sarayi, act. part. of saraya; səruy (K*), pass. part. of saraya; säryat, säryat, səret 'remission of sins, forgive-ness, atonement, absolution, pardon'; soryatäwi (M) 'pertaining to forgiveness'; 'astasrayo 'atonement'; masrayi (K*) 'who forgives, who pardons'; mäsräy (Gr.166) 'place where one pardons'; mastasray, mastasri, mastasräyi (fem. mastasrit), act. part. of 'astasraya;

meaning: 'army, troops, company, legion, host, (Lt) flock, military leader, virile, robust'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sariyyä 'military detachment', SAr. s.r.wyt 'campaigning force' (considered a Geez loan-word by W. Müller, ZDMG 131[1981].402). Bohl 863 compares Ar. sirwa 'arrowhead', and Heb. širyā. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säräwā 'army', Amh. särwe 'leader'; passed into Cushitic: Aw. säräbi, Mo. šäräyäto, Kam. šärawita.

meaning: place of intercession, place of forgiveness, (Gr.166) chapel
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sarā (srw) 'remove', Heb. šārā (šry) 'let loose', Aram.-Syr. šārā 'loosen, absolve', Ug. šry (de Moor 150). ETHIOPIC: Tna. säräyä 'pardon', Amh. (tä)särräyä 'be forgiven'.

meaning: 'les mots, dont les malakkǝt (musical notations) alphabétiques sont des abréviations' (Velat 107ff).
Amh. lämd awäṭṭa 'skin an animal and use its skin in making a lämd-coat'
origin_explanation: The Amharic translation of the verb should read lämä awäṭṭa 'be a leper', lit. 'bring out leprosy', as is the case of sore'ema on the preceding line in Tayyä's vocabulary (p.102). Dillmann 1402 (under swarim) suggests that the Ethiopian lexicographer derived the verb from swarim, above. See also sar'ama.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. soryon, probably from Geez.
from Amh. siso.
from the sounds so so repeated in the songs; see so, above.

meaning: 'that is crumbled, that is broken into small pieces, that is grated'
origin_explanation: either a misprint for sǝkak (see sǝkak, above), or coming from an original saksak > sǝssak, a reduplicated root skk (see sǝkak, above).

meaning: 'withdraw (intr.), recoil, recede, pass away, be removed, depart, be separated'
'putting off, leaving, receding, departure, withdrawal, removal, abolition'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: sǝssala, from slsl, and cp. Ar. salla 'draw, draw out, pull out', Heb. šālal 'draw out (sheaves)'.
Amh. sǝssalä 'be removed, be taken away' (probably from Geez).
note that T.M. and Sw. in D 1395 render it 'be left over, abound' (also sǝsan 'excess'). It would then mean originally 'be in excess, be excessive', with the noun 'excess, impudence'.
sǝsan, sosan, sasne, sǝsn, sǝsan 'lascivious' (Gr.409) impudence, excess, (Lt) divination, cery'.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. sǝssänä, sǝssänä 'be lascivious, lustful' (perhaps from Geez). For 'abound', cp. Tna. sǝssänä 'augment, increase'. A distinction perhaps be made between sǝssana I 'be lascivious' and sǝssana II 'practice divination'.
(that is the tree from which the anthelmintic medicine is made); CUSHITIC: probably Qem. sisähina.

it is strange that T 133 has sallata = Amh. täzäbäṭä 'mock', and the following entry sasata also = Amh. täzäbäbäṭä 'mock'. One wonders whether sasata is not a misprint for sal(l)ata, above. True, Y 332 also has G. sasata = Amh. täzäbäbäṭä, but he may have copied it from Tayyä.
a secondary form of sesaya, below.

meaning: 'move, walk, walk about, go about, take a walk, stroll, go through'
origin_explanation: nasosaw 'walk, walking, strolling, going about'; ansosäwo(t) 'walking, promenade, journeying'; mansosaw, part. of 'ansosawa.

SEMITIC: Ar. šawšä 'nimbly pacing' (Armbruster 964), Heb. šiše 'teach to walk' (Dillmann 385), Littmann, ZA 13 (1899), 155, n. 1 connects it with Heb. sūs 'horse', Syr. sūsyā, Akk. sīsū. This noun, however, is considered a "mot voyageur" (cp. Sans. knt aswas, Egyptian ssm.t). ETHIOPIC: Gur. säwšäw bälä 'roam'. Conti 1980:89, 112 connects the G. verb with G. 'ǝnsǝsä 'animal' and säwšäw 'ladder'.

meaning: 'feed, procure food, nourish, sustain, provide for, support, maintain'
origin_explanation: tǝsäyaya, passive; also 'nourish oneself, eat'; sǝsäyi, act. part. of sesaya; sǝsuy, pass. participle; sisäy 'food, victuals, nourishment, sustenance, maintenance, stipend'; sisit, same; mǝsesäy, part. of sesaya; mǝsǝsit 'nourishment, food'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. säsäyä 'feed', sisäy 'food', Amh. sisäy 'daily bread' (from Geez) passed into Cushitic: Kham. sisäy. See also sǝs(s)awa.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. säṭṭi 'rush'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. säṭṭara 'pay duties', säṭṭarit 'duty, tribute'.

meaning: 'wither, become dry, be curled up like a dry leaf'
'associate with, share, participate, partake, take part in, have fellowship, consort, be a companion to, join in'
'companion, partaker, partner, participator, accomplice, associate, one who shares'
'companion, associate, consort, partner; participation, partnership, communion, participation in the holy sacrament'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Pbh. šiṭṭep 'associate with', Aram. šaṭap, Syr. šawṭep 'associate, make a partner', Md. šip, Akk. šuṭāpu 'companion'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)saṭäfä 'participate', Amh. (tä)saṭṭafä. Noeldeke 1910:44 (following Schwally, ZDMG 52[1898]135) is of the opinion that G. *säṭafa is an Aram.-Syr. loanword. For the Akkad ian origin of this root, see Zimmern 46. On the Sumerian origin of Akk. šuṭappu 'partner', see Kaufman 105, n.371.
metathesis in relation to soṭläfli, below.
could it be a corrupt form of Gk. sitla (< Latin situla) 'bucket, pail?'; see sotofli, above.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. saṭṭänä 'be fast, be in a hurry', Tigre saṭṭänä 'be quick, be fast'.

SEMITIC: Ar. satara 'hide', Sh. Heb. sātar, Aramaic Syr. sātar, Md. Syr. on Ug. str, see Caquot 218, note 1. ETHIOPIC: Te. sātra 'hide', Tna. sāttāra, Amh. sāttāra, Gur. sətər 'hidden place', Har. sutri 'secluded place'. passed into Cushitic: Be. sətir 'hide', Bil. satar. See also satara.

meaning: 'spread out in the sun to dry'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. sāttārā 'spread out in the sun to dry, stretch a hide'.

meaning: 'do something carelessly, do slackly, relax, neglect, disregard, despise, slight, regard lightly, overlook, omit, forfeit'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: For the meaning 'do slackly, be negligent', cp. Tna. sāttātā 'be tired'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. sattātā 'be level, be even', (tān)sattātā 'be flat'.

SEMITIC: Ar. m-s,ty 'drink' (n.), Heb. sāṭā (šty) 'drink' (v.), Aram. šāṭā, Syr. ˀešī, Md. šṭa, Ug. šty, Akk. šatū. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāṭāyā 'drink', Te. sāṭa, Har. sāče, Sāča, Gur. sāče, Amh. māste 'drinking cup' (from Geez), passed into Cushitic: Be. sit 'beverage, broth'.

SEMITIC: Ar. m-s,ty 'drink' (n.), Heb. sāṭā (šty) 'drink' (v.), Aram. šāṭā, Syr. ˀešī, Md. šṭa, Ug. šty, Akk. šatū. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāṭāyā 'drink', Te. sāṭa, Har. sāče, Sāča, Gur. sāče, Amh. māste 'drinking cup' (from Geez), passed into Cushitic: Be. sit 'beverage, broth'.

meaning: 'place of drinking, watering place, watering trough'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. m-s,ty 'drink' (n.), Heb. sāṭā (šty) 'drink' (v.), Aram. šāṭā, Syr. ˀešī, Md. šṭa, Ug. šty, Akk. šatū. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāṭāyā 'drink', Te. sāṭa, Har. sāče, Sāča, Gur. sāče, Amh. māste 'drinking cup' (from Geez), passed into Cushitic: Be. sit 'beverage, broth'.
perhaps from the original meaning 'be thin' (cp. Amh. qāč̣č̣əne 'giraffe', from qṭm 'be thin'), from ṭaṭa 'tear up, rip up > become thin'.
seems to be instead of suṭāb, or the other way around.
see also suṭā, above.

meaning: 'spread, spread out, ex-tend, stretch out, uncover, expose'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. saṭaḥa 'spread', Heb. sāṭaḥ, Aram.-Syr. šaṭaḥ, Akk. šeṭū. ETHIOPIC: Te. sāṭḥa 'spread', Tna. sāṭḥe, Amh.Gur. (a) sāṭa.
from Ar. saṭḥ.

meaning: 'submerge, immerse, inundate, sink, plunge, drown, flood, (K*) cover, swallow, absorb, (Y) destroy'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 400 compares Ar. ˀusṭumm 'sea, depth'. Fraenkel 231 expresses hesitation concerning the unusual form ˀusṭumm alternating with ˀusṭum but the connection between the Geez root sṭm and Heb. sāṭap 'wash' (with alternance of labials) sug-gested by Fraenkel, ib., is unlikely for semantic reasons. Brockelmann 1928:468 connects the Geez root with sṭm (Aram.Syr.Ar.) 'close'; cp. the mean-ing of ˀasṭama 'cover > swallow, submerge'. The root sṭm in the meaning 'close' is related to Heb. sāṭam, Md. sdm. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāṭāmā 'be submerged, drown', Amh. sāmmāṭā, sāṭāmā, Gur. sāmmāṭā (with me-tathesis), salāmāṭā (enlarged by l).
perhaps developed from ˀasṭama 'absorb' (see *saṭma, above).
probably a transcription of Heb. šitṭīm (< šinṭīm) 'acacia'. Note that Heb. šitṭā (pl. šitṭīm), as well as Ar. sant and Akk. samtu go back to Egyptian šnd (Lambdin, JAOS 73[1953].154).

meaning: 'coarse fiber of flax, (T.M) vine, vine shoot'
origin_explanation: from Gk. stypeion 'coarse fiber of flax'. Note that Ar. ˀusṭubba 'coarse fiber of flax' is considered by Wehr 20 an Italian (stoppa) loanword.
from Gk. staktē 'oil of myrrh'; also in Syr. soṭaqṭā, Aramaic sṭaqṭon (Krauss 379). See also suqāṭis, ˀasqa-ṭin.
ETHIOPIC: Te. sāṭra 'hide, keep a secret', a variant of satara I.
reflects either the acc.sg. strobiḷon, or the gen.pl. strobiḷon, of Gk. strobiḷos 'pine'; also Syr. ˀisṭrubiḷyā, Aram. ˀisṭrobiḷon, ˀisṭrobel 'kernel of pine'. See also saṭrappiḷon.

meaning: 'kind of tree called wanza in Amharic'
origin_explanation: see also saṭrobiḷon, saṭrappiḷyon.

meaning: '= Amh. šənāṭ 'a fragrant tree that grows in the lowlands and moderate highlands''
origin_explanation: M renders it 'two-edged knife'. It is true that Amh. šənāṭ also means 'two-edged knife', but in view of saṭrappiḷon = Amh. wanza (see above), it is likely that saṭrappiḷyon is also a kind of tree.
see also sagsas.

phonetics: saṭaṭirā, saṭaṭirā, saṭaṭirās, (L) saṭaṭir, (T.K) saṭaṭirā, (T*) saṭaṭirās
origin_explanation: from Gk. statēr 'a coin', statēra (acc. sg.), statēras (acc. pl.); also Syr. ˀasṭir, Md. asṭira. See also ˀasṭār.

meaning: 'accept, receive, take, answer, respond, give heed, hearken, comply with'
'spread out, extend'; tasatya (T), passive; satuy (KG) 'spread, extended';
origin_explanation: related to satḥa, above.

phonetics: sawā ሰዋ (Lt), sewā ሰዋ (T.M), sāwā ሣዋ (Lt)
meaning: 'drink fermented from corn or barley, the ge-šo-plant used for fermenting beer';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. sawa 'kind of beer', Amh. sewa 'the gešo-plant'.

meaning: 'turn, turn around, wrap around, make something round';
origin_explanation: is related to sbb III.

meaning: 'stir, rake, poke a fire, light (a fire)'; maswad (pl. masāwəd) 'means of stirring a fire, fire pan, fire shovel, fire holder, censer';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: SAr. (s₂wd), ms₂wd rendered by Conti Rossini 1931:254 'saccellum vel ara pro igneo sacrificio' is rendered in SabDic 139 'hall, audience chamber', and is not to be connected with G. (swd), maswad.
from Ar. sawdā' 'bile, melancholy', lit. 'black'. See also aswad.

meaning: 'begin to ripen (fruit or cereal)'; maswal (M) 'fruit or cereal that begins to ripen'.
'cause to separate, make dissolve, make urinate, yawn, gape';
origin_explanation: the basic meaning is that of dissolving or being separated. However, aswana in the meaning 'make urinate' is perhaps a secondary form of syn. šena 'urinate'.
undoubtedly a misprint for sāwā ሳዋ ኀው ሳው, sāwa ሳዋ; see šw.
perhaps a misprint in relation to kopu, above; sopa would then be reconstructed from the noun sopu.

meaning: 'support, prop, protect, sustain, (K) help, encourage'; sawwaqa 'hold together, confine, (MA) heal'; asoqa (K*), causative; tasawaqa (K*), passive of soqa; tasāwaqa 'support one another, support self'; sawāqi, act. part. of soqa; səwwāq (K*) 'that is supported, that is prop up'; soq (LtT) 'prop, support, stake, foundation'; səwwāq, sawāq (pl. *aswāq) 'support, stake'; maswāq 'place of support'; maswaq, (K*) masoq (pl. masāwəq, maswāqt) 'buttress';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 387 compares it with Ar. sāq 'leg, foreleg', Heb. šōq 'foreleg', Syr šāqā. ETHIOPIC: Amh. soqā 'prop up' (KWK 853, swq) probably from Geez.
from Ar. sawāqi', pl. of sāqiya. See also saqaya.

meaning: 'hide, conceal, cover over, shield, screen, protect'; tasawwara, passive, reflexive; 'also vanish, disappear'; sawwāri, act. part. of sawwara; səwwar, səwwur 'hidden, concealed, covered, secret, obscure'; sawwārət (pl. of səwwər) 'mysteries';
origin_explanation: səwwəre 'concealment, hiding (n.)'. masawwər, (KG) masāwwər 'protector, lid, cover, blanket'; masəwwər 'hiding place, hidden place, secret place, shelter, refuge, recourse, cover, covering, protection, entrenchment'; SEMITIC: Aram. swyr 'hide' (Driver 1935:55). Dillmann 384 suggests Ar. srr, sārra 'confide a secret'. Rundgren 1955:26 derives it from wr 'be blind' with the causative morpheme s agglutinated to the root. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. sāwwārā 'hide', Te. sāwwārā, Gur. siwwārā.
from Ar. tawr. See also sor I.

meaning: 'ladder, step of a ladder, grammar, glossary, vocabulary'; sawsawa (T.Y.M) 'make a ladder' (denominative); sawsāsāwī (K) 'grammarian, grammatical';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. sāwāsaw 'grammar, vocabulary'. Dillmann 386 connects it with 'an-sosawa 'move, walk', so also Conti 1980:112. The concept and the term sawsāw are taken from the Coptic Arabic term for grammar, namely sullam 'ladder'. The most detailed study on the contents and the grammatical terminology of the Sawsāw is found in M.M. Moreno, "Struttura e terminologia del Sawsāw", RSE 8 (1949).12-62. Moreno, ib. gives a bibliography of the studies dealing with the Sawsāw as well as with the sullam.

from Ar. (Yemen) šataleh 'fork', of Turkish (čatal) origin; also in Te. šōtāl, šōtāl 'dagger', Tna. šātāl, šōtāl, šōtāl, Amh. šōtāl, Har. šōtāl; in Cushitic: Som. šōtāl 'big awl', Bil. šōtāl 'dagger'. Dem. šutāl, Sa. šōtāl.

phonetics: swt I, sota (yəsuṭ) ሰጠ (T.Y) šoṭa ሾጠ, sawata ሰወጠ (KG)
meaning: 'pour, spill, diffuse, infuse, instill, spread, shed, empty, drain, turn over, change'; (soṭa rə'so 'he divested himself of'); sawwaṭa 'pour, pour out, (T.Y.M) change, substitute'; asoṭa, causative;
origin_explanation: tasoṭa, tasawwaṭa 'be poured, be poured out, be emptied (into), be spread'; sawwəṭ, sawwuṭ, pass. part. of soṭa; suṭat, verbal noun of soṭa; asoṭa 'evacuation, divestiture'; sawwəṭa 'evacuation, emptying'; SEMITIC: Ar. sawṭ 'place where water collects', SAr. syt 'rainwater cisterns' (Müller 1983:283), Heb. šōṭ 'spate'. For the Arabic root, see also Barth 1893:14; ZAW 34 (1914). 68. Ahrens 1930:23, following J. Horowitz, derives Ar. sawṭ from the Geez root. See also swṭ II.

phonetics: swt II, soṭa (yəsuṭ) ሰጠ (K), šoṭa ሾጠ (T.Y.M)
meaning: 'add, unite, mix, insert, put under the arm'; sawata (Lt) 'bring in, mix together, confuse, confound'; tasawaṭa (K*), passive; tasāwaṭa 'support one another, support self'; sawāṭi, act. part. of soṭa; səwwəṭ 'heap, mixed, added'; suṭat (K*), verbal noun of soṭa; masoṭ 'mixing-vessel';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sāṭa (swṭ) 'mix, stir'; perhaps to be identified with soṭa I 'pour > add'.
SEMITIC: Ar. sāṭa (swṭ) 'flagellate', sawṭ 'whip'; Heb. šōṭ, Aram.-Syr. šawṭā. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāwəṭa 'flog', Tna. sāwwāṭā, Amh. sāwṭ 'whip' (from Arabic); also šoṭṭ adārrāgā 'whip' (v), Har. sāṭ āsa.
see say!, below.

meaning: 'commit a disgraceful or depraved act, be given to fornication'; saya>, (L) šaya 'disgraceful, scandalous, or depraved act';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sā'a (sw') 'become bad, evil, deteriorate', SAr. s₂y' 'bad, wicked', Heb. šāw' 'worthless', šw (hif-il) 'treat badly', Ug. š'iy 'criminal' (Caquot 439, n. c). The comparison with Akk. šūṭu 'south wind' suggested by Fronzaroli 1965, III:146 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sāyā-et 'she became a woman of ill repute'. See also šyy.

From Ar. sayyida 'lady'; also in Tna.Amh. sayda. The meaning 'pity, pardon' is explained by Guidi in Noeldeke 1906:920 as an allegoric interpretation. The term saydā refers to the Virgin Mary, since she is considered the intercessor.
the form is enigmatic.
From Chr.-Ar. sayfih 'itinerant dervish'; the verb is a denominative.

From Ar. sayf 'sword' (for its Aramaic origin, see Fraenkel 239, A. Fischer 1953:61); also in Te.Amh. sāyf. Tna. sāyfi, Har. sif, Gur. sef; in Cushitic: Bil.Som. sef, Kham. sif. The Greek origin (ksiphos) suggested by Vollers, ZDMG 51 (1897).298 is unlikely.

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'melt, liquefy, dissolve, pound, crush, (M) strain, percolate';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. sāha (syh) 'melt' (Dozy), Heb. sāh (šyh) 'be reduced to dust', Syr. sāh (šwh) 'melt, waste away'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. sayyah 'one who makes special objects at the court (gold and silver cups, and others)'. See also sayha II.

meaning: 'go bad, languish, be sick, die, be lost, be dry, be moldy';
origin_explanation: the meanings of this root are probably figurative meanings of sayha I. For the passage of meanings,
cp. Latin tabescere 'melt gradually, be dissolved, waste, pine, dwindle away, languish', same. See also sayyāh.
sayyāh (T.D 1395) 'monk, ascetic, hermit (possibly having the role of soothsayer)'; sayyāhāt, pl. sayyāhāt (D 393) 'divination, act(s) of divination'.
SEMITIC: perhaps Akk. šāhu 'bowl, basin'.
perhaps to be connected with sayha II.
from say 'woe' (see above) and agglutinated l 'to' for l agglutinated to an interjection, cp. also wayle 'misfortune, woe!', from way 'woe!', and l 'to' woe!'.

SEMITIC: Ar. salā 'membrane encasing the foetus', Heb. šilyā 'afterbirth', Aram. šilyātā, Syr. šəlitā, Akk. šilitu, šelitu. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. šəl 'foetus', Gur. šəl, šər 'embryo'; connected with Tna. šalāt 'placenta', Te. passed into Cushitic: Bil. silēt.
From Ar. sayqal, sayqal: for the Arabic forms, see Fraenkel 254.

SEMITIC: G. saytān (saytān) is taken from Aram. sāṭānā (Heb. śāṭān); see Noeldeke 1910:34. As for the relationship between G. saytān and Ar. šayṭān, Praetorius in ZDMG 61 (1901).619ff thinks that saytān is an Arabic loanword and states that "der jüdisch-christliche saytān durch arabische Beeinflussung entstanden ist". Noeldeke 1910:47 is of the opinion that Ar. šayṭān is a Geez loanword (so A. Fischer 1953:66). The Arabic-Geez form šayṭān (with ay) against Heb. śāṭān (with ā) explained by Praetorius, ZDMG 72 (1918).343-4 a pejorative form ('schlimmer Satan'), a form he finds in Ar. (Datina) nusayn from insān. For bibliography on saytān, see Praetorius, ZDMG (1907).619ff. ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. šäyṭān, Te. šəṭān.
Sw. in D 1402 renders səzn with səndāle 'fine flour', for which Dillmann ib. suggests sandal 'sandalwood'.

SEMITIC: Soq. śəzir, Mh. hazer (h = s). The connection between G. səzər and Heb. zereq, Syr. zar-tā (Add Ug. dr-t) was suggested by Halévy, REJ 11 (1885).60ff, and also by Dillmann 392. Hebrew zereq, on the other hand, is considered an Egyptian loanword (Bondi, ZAS 52, 1984, p. 132, and Lambdin, JAOS 73, 1953, p. 143ff). The derivation of G. səzər from *sənē 'qsr' 'twelve' > sənəšər > sənṣər > səzər, suggested by Rundgren 1961:369 (even if supported by Greek spithame 'Handspanne, drei palaistai, um je vier Finger', d.h. zusammen zwölf Finger'), is unlikely. The connection between G. səzər and Ar. šibr 'span' which Praetorius 1890: 44-5 suggests, with reservations, is not convincing. Praetorius, ZDMG 53 (1899).348 compares G. səzər with Ar. šazara 'twist (thread)'. The connection with Akk. sadāru, sedēru 'draw up in military order', suggested by Rabin, SH 8 (1961).393, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. sənzārā, sədədārā (with alternation d:z) 'measure with the span', Te. sədədāra, Amh. sənzāzārā, Arg. sənzāzāra, denominatives of Tna. sədri, Te. sədər, Amh.Arg. sənzər, Har. zunzurii, Gur. sənzər; probably passed into Cushitic: Had. zənzənra.
